title: Ensure tab completion works with installed brew package
description: Verify and ensure that shell tab completion works correctly when dogcat is installed via Homebrew (brew). This includes testing that completions are properly registered and functional for the dcat CLI.

title: Add worktree field to Issue data model
description: Add an optional worktree field to the Issue dataclass in models.py to track which git worktree an issue belongs to. This would allow assigning and filtering issues by worktree when multiple worktrees share the same .dogcats directory.

title: Set up dcat web command with FastAPI + Jinja2 + Tailwind skeleton
description: Create the dcat web subcommand (feature-gated behind DCAT_FEATURE_WEB). Set up FastAPI app with Jinja2 templating and Tailwind CSS via CDN. Bind to 127.0.0.1:8000 by default. Add FastAPI and Jinja2 as optional dependencies. Create base template with Tailwind CDN link, navigation layout, and project name header.
acceptance:
- dcat web starts a FastAPI server on 127.0.0.1:8000
- Feature-gated: requires DCAT_FEATURE_WEB=1
- Base HTML template with Tailwind CDN and nav layout
- / redirects to /issues
- --port and --host flags supported
notes: Mobile-first responsive design. Use Tailwind responsive utilities (sm/md/lg breakpoints). Primarily tested on mobile viewports.

title: Issue list view
description: Read-only issue list page showing all issues in a filterable table. Reuse storage.py to load issues. Support filtering by status, type, priority, and labels via query params. Show columns: ID, title, type, status, priority, labels, updated_at.
acceptance:
- /issues route renders a list of all issues
- Filters for status, type, priority, labels
- Sortable by priority and updated_at
- Links to issue detail view
notes: Mobile-friendly layout: stack filters vertically on small screens, compact table or card-based list on mobile.

title: Kanban board view
description: Read-only kanban board showing issues as cards grouped by status columns (Open, In Progress, In Review, Blocked, Deferred). Each card shows issue ID, title, type badge, and priority indicator. Cards link to issue detail view.
acceptance:
- /kanban route renders a kanban board
- Columns for each active status
- Issue cards with key info
- Cards link to detail view
- Responsive layout with horizontal scroll if needed
notes: Mobile-friendly kanban: consider vertical column stacking or horizontal swipe on small screens.

title: Issue detail view
description: Read-only detail page for a single issue. Shows all fields: title, description, status, priority, type, labels, owner, acceptance criteria, design notes, notes, comments, dependencies (blocks/blocked-by), parent/children, external ref, timestamps. Render markdown in description and notes fields.
acceptance:
- /issues/{issue_id} route renders full issue details
- All issue fields displayed
- Dependencies shown with links to related issues
- Parent/children shown with links
- Comments listed chronologically
- Markdown rendered in text fields
notes: Mobile-friendly detail layout: single-column, readable on small screens.

title: Dependency graph visualization
description: Visual dependency graph showing issue relationships. Consider using a JS graph library (e.g., D3, dagre, mermaid) to render blocks/blocked-by and parent/child relationships.

title: History/timeline view
description: View showing issue change history and timeline. Reuse event log data from storage to show a chronological feed of issue changes across the project.

title: Dashboard/summary stats view
description: Dashboard page with summary statistics: issue counts by status, type, priority. Burn-down style metrics. Overview of project health.

title: Replace Tailwind CDN with standalone CLI build
description: Replace Tailwind CSS CDN link with the standalone Tailwind CLI for offline support and smaller CSS output. Bundle the compiled CSS with the package.

title: File-watching event stream library
description: Internal library that watches issues.jsonl for changes and emits events. Uses watchdog for filesystem monitoring. Exposes a Python API (not CLI) that consumers (web UI, future CLI command) can import. Lives in src/dogcat/stream/ or similar.

title: Read-write support for Web UI
description: Add write capabilities to the Web UI: create issues, update fields, change status, close issues, add comments. Requires CSRF protection and form validation. Reuse storage.py write methods.

title: Multi-project support in Web UI
description: Support loading .dogcats/ folders from multiple repositories simultaneously. Add a project switcher in the nav. Single web server instance serves all configured projects. Configuration for project paths (CLI args or config file).

title: Wire live updates into Web UI via SSE
description: Add SSE endpoint to the web server that uses the internal file-watching stream library (dogcat-3tuf) to push issue change events to the browser. Add JS client-side listener to auto-refresh views without polling. Should import the library directly, not shell out to a CLI command.

title: Web UI test infrastructure and tests
description: Set up test infrastructure for the web UI using httpx AsyncClient as the FastAPI test client. Write tests for all routes: /, /issues, /issues/{id}, /kanban. Test filtering, sorting, error handling (missing issues, invalid filters). Test template rendering produces expected HTML structure. Add to the existing pytest suite.
acceptance:
- Test client fixture using httpx AsyncClient
- Route tests for all endpoints
- Filter and sort parameter tests
- Error case tests (404 for missing issues, bad params)
- Template content assertions
- Integrated into just test

title: Web UI for dogcat
description: Server-side rendered web dashboard for dogcat issue tracking. FastAPI + Jinja2 + Tailwind CSS (CDN). No SPA. Listens on 127.0.0.1 by default. Designed for desktop and mobile, with a mobile-first approach — primarily tested on mobile. Initial version is read-only. Future phases: read-write support, multi-project support, live updates.
acceptance:
- dcat web launches a local web server on 127.0.0.1
- Issue list view with filtering
- Kanban board view
- Issue detail view
- Feature-gated behind DCAT_FEATURE_WEB

Fix -n short flag conflict between --limit and --namespace
description:
The -n short flag is used for --limit in recently-closed and recently-added, but -n is reserved for --namespace (per the -n shorthand proposal dogcat-oq90). This creates an inconsistency across the CLI.

Affected commands using -n for --limit:
- recently-closed (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1215)
- recently-added (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1281)

Additionally, -n is also overloaded for:
- --notes in create (_cmd_create.py:153) and update (_cmd_update.py:85)
- --dry-run in prune (_cmd_admin.py:34)

Fix: Remove -n as a short flag from --limit in recently-closed and recently-added. Consider also auditing --notes and --dry-run usages of -n for consistency.

Add dcat git rebase command to auto-resolve JSONL merge conflicts
description:
Add a `dcat git rebase` command that detects and auto-resolves merge conflicts in `.dogcats/*.jsonl` files (issues.jsonl and inbox.jsonl) using the existing semantic merge logic in merge_driver.py.

**Behavior:**
1. Scan `.dogcats/issues.jsonl` and `.dogcats/inbox.jsonl` for git conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`)
2. Extract base/ours/theirs sections from the conflicted file
3. Run `merge_jsonl()` to produce a clean resolved result
4. Write the resolved file and `git add` it
5. If no conflicts found, print a message and exit

**Does NOT perform any git operations** (no pull, no rebase, no continue) — it only resolves the JSONL conflicts. The user handles the git workflow themselves.

**Typical workflow:**
```
git pull --rebase
# conflict in .dogcats/issues.jsonl
dcat git rebase
# resolved
git rebase --continue
```
acceptance:
- Resolves conflict markers in issues.jsonl using semantic merge logic
- Resolves conflict markers in inbox.jsonl using semantic merge logic
- Runs git add on resolved files
- Reports which files were resolved
- Reports when no conflicts are found
- Has tests

Fix -n short flag conflict between --limit and --namespace
description:
The -n short flag is used for --limit in recently-closed and recently-added, but -n is reserved for --namespace (per the -n shorthand proposal dogcat-oq90). This creates an inconsistency across the CLI.

Affected commands using -n for --limit:
- recently-closed (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1215)
- recently-added (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1281)

Additionally, -n is also overloaded for:
- --notes in create (_cmd_create.py:153) and update (_cmd_update.py:85)
- --dry-run in prune (_cmd_admin.py:34)

Fix: Remove -n as a short flag from --limit in recently-closed and recently-added. Consider also auditing --notes and --dry-run usages of -n for consistency.

dcat doctor: detect and fix missing Claude Code hooks (PreCompact/SessionStart)
description:
After context compaction, agents lose workflow context and become braindead. Claude Code supports PreCompact and SessionStart hooks that can re-inject workflow context.

**What**: Add a dcat doctor check that:
1. Detects if .claude/settings.json (or settings.local.json) exists
2. Checks whether PreCompact and SessionStart hooks are registered to run `dcat prime`
3. If missing, warns the user and offers to install them automatically

**Hook config to install** (in .claude/settings.json or settings.local.json):
```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "SessionStart": [{
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "dcat prime" }]
    }],
    "PreCompact": [{
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "dcat prime" }]
    }]
  }
}
```

**Why**: beads does exactly this — `bd prime` on both SessionStart and PreCompact. The PreCompact hook injects context right before the compactor summarizes, so workflow instructions survive compaction. SessionStart re-injects after compaction completes.

**Acceptance criteria**:
- dcat doctor reports missing hooks as a warning (not a failure — not all users use Claude Code)
- When hooks are missing, doctor asks if user wants to fix it
- Fix installs hooks into .claude/settings.local.json (not committed to repo) if it exists, otherwise .claude/settings.json
- Merges with existing hooks/settings rather than overwriting
- If hooks are already present, check passes silently
- Works whether dcat is installed globally or via uv

title: dcat doctor: detect and fix missing Claude Code hooks (PreCompact/SessionStart)
dcat doctor: detect and fix missing Claude Code PreCompact hook
description:
After context compaction, agents lose workflow context and become braindead. Claude Code supports a PreCompact hook that injects context right before the compactor summarizes, so workflow instructions survive compaction.

**What**: Add a dcat doctor check that:
1. Detects if .claude/ directory exists (skip check entirely if not — not all users use Claude Code)
2. Checks whether a PreCompact hook is registered to run `dcat prime` in settings.json or settings.local.json
3. If missing, warns the user and offers to install it automatically

**Hook config to install**:
```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreCompact": [{
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "dcat prime" }]
    }]
  }
}
```

**Why PreCompact only (no SessionStart)**: dcat is optional — most repos won't have .dogcats/. A SessionStart hook would run on every Claude Code session and error/noise in non-dogcat repos. Session start is already handled by the CLAUDE.md instruction 'always run dcat prime'. PreCompact is the one case CLAUDE.md can't cover, since the agent forgets it after compaction.

**Prior art**: beads does this with `bd prime` on both hooks, but beads is less optional (it owns the project).

**Acceptance criteria**:
- dcat doctor skips the check entirely if .claude/ directory doesn't exist
- Reports missing PreCompact hook as a warning (not a failure)
- When hook is missing, doctor asks if user wants to fix it
- Fix installs hook into .claude/settings.local.json (preferred, gitignored) or .claude/settings.json
- Merges with existing hooks/settings rather than overwriting
- If hook is already present, check passes silently

description:
After context compaction, agents lose workflow context and become braindead. Claude Code supports a PreCompact hook that injects context right before the compactor summarizes, so workflow instructions survive compaction.

**What**: Add a dcat doctor check that:
1. Detects if .claude/ directory exists (skip check entirely if not — not all users use Claude Code)
2. Checks whether a PreCompact hook is registered to run `dcat prime` in settings.json or settings.local.json
3. If missing, warns the user and offers to install it automatically

**Hook config to install**:
```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreCompact": [{
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "dcat prime" }]
    }]
  }
}
```

**Why PreCompact only (no SessionStart)**: dcat is optional — most repos won't have .dogcats/. A SessionStart hook would run on every Claude Code session and error/noise in non-dogcat repos. Session start is already handled by the CLAUDE.md instruction 'always run dcat prime'. PreCompact is the one case CLAUDE.md can't cover, since the agent forgets it after compaction.

**Prior art**: beads does this with `bd prime` on both hooks, but beads is less optional (it owns the project).

**Acceptance criteria**:
- dcat doctor skips the check entirely if .claude/ directory doesn't exist
- Reports missing PreCompact hook as a warning (not a failure)
- When hook is missing, doctor asks if user wants to fix it
- Fix installs hook into .claude/settings.local.json (preferred, gitignored) or .claude/settings.json
- Merges with existing hooks/settings rather than overwriting
- If hook is already present, check passes silently

title: dcat doctor: detect and fix missing Claude Code PreCompact hook

PreCompact hook doesn't preserve --opinionated flag
description: The PreCompact hook installed by dcat doctor --fix hardcodes 'dcat prime' without --opinionated. If the user started the session with dcat prime --opinionated, the stricter rules are lost after compaction. Fix: add a prime_opinionated config key that makes bare 'dcat prime' behave as --opinionated when set.

Agents skip 'create issue first' rule for small/obvious tasks
description: CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'If the user brings up a new bug, feature or anything else that warrants changes to the code, ALWAYS ask if we should create an issue for it before you start working on the code.' Despite this, agents routinely skip this step when the task feels small or obvious — rationalizing that it's 'just two lines' or 'too trivial for an issue'. This defeats the purpose of the rule, which exists for traceability and discipline regardless of task size. The --opinionated prime output should reinforce this more forcefully. Possible approaches: add a dedicated rule in the opinionated section that explicitly calls out this failure mode, reword to make it harder to rationalize away, or make it a top-level rule rather than buried in prose.

title.new: Agents skip 'create issue first' rule for small/obvious tasks

Support multiple issue IDs in dcat update
description: Allow dcat update to accept multiple issue IDs (like close/reopen already do). When multiple IDs are provided, apply the same updates to all of them. Guard against combining multiple IDs with single-issue options (--title, --description, --parent, --duplicate-of, --namespace) by erroring early. Common use case: dcat update --status in_progress issue1 issue2 issue3.

Add exhaustiveness check for unknown record types in classify_record()
description:
classify_record() in src/dogcat/models.py defaults to 'issue' for any unrecognized record_type value. If a new record type is added in the future without updating the merge driver, those records get misclassified and merged with wrong semantics — potentially causing silent data loss during merges.

Fix: Add an exhaustiveness check that warns or raises on unknown record types instead of silently defaulting to 'issue'. The merge driver (src/dogcat/merge_driver.py) relies on classify_record() at lines 207, 223, 251, 271, 297.

Files: src/dogcat/models.py:468-482, src/dogcat/merge_driver.py

title.new: Add exhaustiveness check for unknown record types in classify_record()

Fix: Add an exhaustiveness check that warns or raises on unknown record types instead of silently defaulting to 'issue'. The merge driver (src/dogcat/merge_driver.py) relies on classify_record() at lines 207, 223, 251, 271, 297.

Add warning logs for malformed records in merge conflict sections
description:
parse_conflicted_jsonl() in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py uses contextlib.suppress(orjson.JSONDecodeError) with no logging at lines 81 and 96 inside conflict sections. Data can be silently lost during merge with zero trace.

The non-conflict parser _parse_jsonl() already logs warnings for malformed lines (line 129-134) — this should be consistent. Malformed records in conflict sections should produce at least a warning log.

Files: src/dogcat/merge_driver.py:81,96 (conflict sections) vs :129-134 (existing warning pattern)

Fix compaction race condition in storage layer
description:
_maybe_compact() in src/dogcat/storage.py checks line counts outside the lock (lines 417-428), then _save() acquires the lock separately. Two concurrent processes can both decide to compact redundantly.

More critically, _maybe_compact() is called after releasing the append lock (line 394), so line counts may be stale between the append completing and the compaction check running.

Fix: Move the compaction eligibility check inside the lock, or call _maybe_compact() within the append lock context.

Files: src/dogcat/storage.py:394,417-428

title.new: Fix compaction race condition in storage layer

description.new:
_maybe_compact() in src/dogcat/storage.py checks line counts outside the lock (lines 417-428), then _save() acquires the lock separately. Two concurrent processes can both decide to compact redundantly.

More critically, _maybe_compact() is called after releasing the append lock (line 394), so line counts may be stale between the append completing and the compaction check running.

Fix: Move the compaction eligibility check inside the lock, or call _maybe_compact() within the append lock context.

Files: src/dogcat/storage.py:394,417-428

title: Fix compaction race condition in storage layer
Add optional per-record checksums to JSONL storage
description:
JSONL records in src/dogcat/storage.py have no embedded CRC or hash. A bit-flip or partial write producing valid-but-incorrect JSON is undetectable. dcat doctor can catch structural issues but not data corruption.

Consider adding an optional record-level checksum (e.g., CRC32 trailer) for a stricter validation mode. This would allow detecting corrupted-but-valid JSON records that currently pass parsing without error.

Files: src/dogcat/storage.py

title.new: Add optional per-record checksums to JSONL storage

description.new:
JSONL records in src/dogcat/storage.py have no embedded CRC or hash. A bit-flip or partial write producing valid-but-incorrect JSON is undetectable. dcat doctor can catch structural issues but not data corruption.

Consider adding an optional record-level checksum (e.g., CRC32 trailer) for a stricter validation mode. This would allow detecting corrupted-but-valid JSON records that currently pass parsing without error.

Files: src/dogcat/storage.py

title: Add optional per-record checksums to JSONL storage
Add rate limiting to web proposal API
description:
The FastAPI proposal endpoint in src/dogcat/web/propose/routes.py has CSRF protection and input length validation (MAX_TITLE_LEN=500, MAX_DESC_LEN=50_000), but no rate limiting. An open localhost endpoint accepting 50KB proposals could be abused if exposed.

Fix: Add basic rate limiting (e.g., slowapi or simple in-memory counter).

Files: src/dogcat/web/propose/routes.py:105-125

Rotate CSRF tokens per session instead of per app start
description:
The CSRF token in src/dogcat/web/propose/__init__.py is generated once at startup via secrets.token_urlsafe(32) and shared across all sessions. Any client that obtains it can reuse it indefinitely until server restart.

Fix: Rotate CSRF tokens per session or add expiry.

Files: src/dogcat/web/propose/__init__.py:66

Add Windows/non-POSIX fallback for file locking
description:
fcntl.flock() is used directly in storage.py, inbox.py, and event_log.py with no platform detection or fallback. On Windows, the import fails with ModuleNotFoundError.

Fix: Add platform detection with msvcrt.locking() fallback, or document POSIX-only requirement explicitly.

Consider command registry pattern for CLI modules
description:
Each _cmd_*.py module in src/dogcat/cli/ independently imports storage, config, IDGenerator, models. No middleware layer or command registry pattern. Tab completion is manually registered per option in _completions.py. Scales poorly past ~50 commands.

Consider a command registry or plugin pattern if growth continues.

Add dcat hook install/uninstall/list commands for Claude Code hooks
description:
Add CLI commands to manage Claude Code hooks in .claude/settings.local.json.

## Commands

### dcat hook install <name> [options]
Installs a named hook into .claude/settings.local.json. Creates the file or merges into existing structure. Idempotent — skips with a message if already present.

Available hooks:
- sessionStart: SessionStart event, matcher 'startup|resume|clear', runs 'dcat prime'. With --opinionated flag, runs 'dcat prime --opinionated' instead.
- preCompact: PreCompact event, matcher '' (all), runs 'dcat prime --replay'.

### dcat hook uninstall <name>
Removes the named hook from .claude/settings.local.json. Cleans up empty hooks/event arrays after removal.

### dcat hook list
Shows installed hooks from .claude/settings.local.json. Also shows hooks from .claude/settings.json marked as '(shared)'.

## Edge cases
- No .claude/settings.local.json exists → create it
- File exists but no hooks key → add it
- Hook already installed → no-op with message
- Uninstall hook that isn't installed → no-op with message

## Tab completions
Add autocompletion callbacks for hook names (sessionStart, preCompact) on install and uninstall subcommands, per project conventions.
acceptance:
- [ ] dcat hook install sessionStart adds SessionStart hook to .claude/settings.local.json
- [ ] dcat hook install sessionStart --opinionated uses 'dcat prime --opinionated'
- [ ] dcat hook install preCompact adds PreCompact hook
- [ ] Installs are idempotent
- [ ] dcat hook uninstall removes hooks and cleans up empty structures
- [ ] dcat hook list shows installed hooks (local and shared)
- [ ] Tab completions for hook names on install/uninstall
- [ ] Tests for all commands

title.new: Add dcat hook install/uninstall/list commands for Claude Code hooks

Support per-repo config.local.toml when using shared database via .dogcatrc
description: When .dogcatrc redirects to a shared .dogcats directory, config.local.toml is only read from the shared location. This means all repos sharing a database cannot have their own default namespace or visible_namespaces. Add support for reading a local config.local.toml from the repo's own .dogcats/ directory (or alongside .dogcatrc), merged on top of the shared config. This enables the multi-repo workflow where each repo sets its own namespace and visibility filters while sharing one issues.jsonl.

storage.update() doesn't clear closed_at when status changes away from closed
description:
When using `dcat update --status <non-closed>` on a closed issue, the status field changes but `closed_at`, `close_reason`, and `closed_by` are not cleared. This causes data inconsistency where an issue shows as e.g. 'in_review' but still has a closed_at timestamp, making it appear in both the in-review list and showing a '[closed ...]' annotation.

Root cause: `storage.update()` is a generic field setter that uses `setattr` without any awareness of status transition side effects. The dedicated `reopen()` method correctly clears `closed_at` (line 1043), but `update()` bypasses that logic entirely.

Conversely, setting status to `closed` via `update()` doesn't populate `closed_at` either — only `storage.close()` does.

Fix: add status transition guards in `storage.update()` after the field-setting loop:
- If status changed away from closed → clear closed_at, close_reason, closed_by
- If status changed to closed → set closed_at to now

Files: `src/dogcat/storage.py` (update method ~line 722), tests in `tests/`
acceptance:
1. `dcat update --status in_review <closed-issue>` clears closed_at, close_reason, closed_by
2. `dcat update --status closed <open-issue>` sets closed_at
3. Existing `close()` and `reopen()` behavior unchanged
4. Tests cover both transitions

title.new: storage.update() doesn't clear closed_at when status changes away from closed

acceptance.new:
1. `dcat update --status in_review <closed-issue>` clears closed_at, close_reason, closed_by
2. `dcat update --status closed <open-issue>` sets closed_at
3. Existing `close()` and `reopen()` behavior unchanged
4. Tests cover both transitions

description.new:
When using `dcat update --status <non-closed>` on a closed issue, the status field changes but `closed_at`, `close_reason`, and `closed_by` are not cleared. This causes data inconsistency where an issue shows as e.g. 'in_review' but still has a closed_at timestamp, making it appear in both the in-review list and showing a '[closed ...]' annotation.

Root cause: `storage.update()` is a generic field setter that uses `setattr` without any awareness of status transition side effects. The dedicated `reopen()` method correctly clears `closed_at` (line 1043), but `update()` bypasses that logic entirely.

Conversely, setting status to `closed` via `update()` doesn't populate `closed_at` either — only `storage.close()` does.

Fix: add status transition guards in `storage.update()` after the field-setting loop:
- If status changed away from closed → clear closed_at, close_reason, closed_by
- If status changed to closed → set closed_at to now

Files: `src/dogcat/storage.py` (update method ~line 722), tests in `tests/`

Add --no-parent filter to dcat list
description: Add a --no-parent flag to the list command that filters to only show issues where parent is None (top-level issues). This is the inverse of the existing --parent flag which filters to a specific parent's children.

title: Notify when closing the last open issue in an epic
Add snooze/postpone feature for issues
description:
Add ability to snooze/postpone issues for a specified duration. Snoozed issues keep their original status (open, in_progress, in_review, etc.) but are hidden from `dcat list` and `dcat ready` output by default.

## Behavior
- Snooze is orthogonal to status — an issue can be snoozed regardless of its current status
- Snoozed issues are excluded from `list` and `ready` unless `--include-snoozed` or `--all` is used
- When the snooze period expires, the issue reappears automatically (no status change needed)
- Users can unsnooze early

## CLI surface
- `dcat snooze <id> <duration>` — snooze for relative duration (7d, 2w, 1m)
- `dcat snooze <id> --until <date>` — snooze until absolute date
- `dcat unsnooze <id>` — clear snooze early
- `dcat snoozed` — list currently snoozed issues with expiry dates
- `dcat update <id> --snooze-until <date>` — alternative via update command

## Data model
- Add `snoozed_until: datetime | None` field to Issue dataclass
- Add to UPDATABLE_FIELDS and TRACKED_FIELDS for audit trail
- Orthogonal to status — no new status enum value needed

## Filtering changes
- `get_ready_work()` in deps.py: exclude issues where snoozed_until > now
- `list` command: exclude snoozed issues from default output
- Add `--include-snoozed` flag to list/ready commands

## Files to touch
- models.py: Add snoozed_until field
- constants.py: Add to TRACKED_FIELDS
- storage.py: Add to UPDATABLE_FIELDS, filtering logic
- deps.py: Exclude snoozed from ready work
- cli/_cmd_read.py: List filtering
- cli/_cmd_workflow.py: New snooze/unsnooze/snoozed commands
- cli/_cmd_update.py: --snooze-until option
- cli/_completions.py: Duration/date completers
- cli/_formatting.py: Snoozed badge in display
- cli/_helpers.py: Common filter updates
acceptance:
- [ ] `dcat snooze <id> 7d` sets snoozed_until to 7 days from now
- [ ] `dcat unsnooze <id>` clears snoozed_until
- [ ] `dcat snoozed` lists currently snoozed issues
- [ ] Snoozed issues hidden from `dcat list` and `dcat ready` by default
- [ ] `--include-snoozed` flag reveals snoozed issues in list/ready
- [ ] Issue retains its original status while snoozed
- [ ] Expired snoozes automatically unhide (no cron needed)
- [ ] Snooze changes tracked in event audit log
- [ ] Tab completions for duration argument
- [ ] Tests for snooze filtering, expiry, and CLI commands

Fix N+1 lookup patterns in deps.py
description: Five functions in deps.py repeatedly call storage.get() and storage.get_dependencies() per issue inside loops/recursion, causing O(n*d) performance. Affected: _has_deferred_ancestor (line 49-60), get_ready_work blocker check (71-88), get_blocked_issues (108-123), detect_cycles DFS (152-180), would_create_cycle (232-242). Fix: pre-build lookup dicts (all_issues_by_id, dep_graph) once before loops; memoize recursive calls.

Add exhaustiveness check for unknown record types in classify_record()
description:
classify_record() in src/dogcat/models.py defaults to 'issue' for any unrecognized record_type value. If a new record type is added in the future without updating the merge driver, those records get misclassified and merged with wrong semantics — potentially causing silent data loss during merges.

Fix: Add an exhaustiveness check that warns or raises on unknown record types instead of silently defaulting to 'issue'. The merge driver (src/dogcat/merge_driver.py) relies on classify_record() at lines 207, 223, 251, 271, 297.

Files: src/dogcat/models.py:468-482, src/dogcat/merge_driver.py

title: Add exhaustiveness check for unknown record types in classify_record()
Add warning logs for malformed records in merge conflict sections
description:
parse_conflicted_jsonl() in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py uses contextlib.suppress(orjson.JSONDecodeError) with no logging at lines 81 and 96 inside conflict sections. Data can be silently lost during merge with zero trace.

The non-conflict parser _parse_jsonl() already logs warnings for malformed lines (line 129-134) — this should be consistent. Malformed records in conflict sections should produce at least a warning log.

Upgrade GitHub Actions to Node.js 24
description:
GitHub is deprecating Node.js 20 in Actions runners. Three actions across our 4 workflow files still run on Node.js 20:

- `actions/checkout@v4` -> upgrade to `@v5`
- `astral-sh/setup-uv@v4` -> upgrade to latest major with Node 24 support
- `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` -> no v3 yet; use `FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true` env var as workaround

**Timeline:** Node.js 24 forced default June 2, 2026. Node.js 20 removed September 16, 2026.

**Affected files:**
- .github/workflows/ci.yml
- .github/workflows/publish.yml
- .github/workflows/release.yml
- .github/workflows/validate-issues.yml
acceptance:
- All workflows use Node.js 24-compatible action versions
- No Node.js 20 deprecation warnings in CI runs
- All workflow runs pass after the upgrade

description.new:

Add --manual filter to all list-style subcommands
description:
Currently `dcat list` (and siblings like `ready`, `blocked`, `pr`, `in-progress`, `in-review`, `deferred`, `stale`, `snoozed`, `recently-added`, `recently-closed`, `open`, `search`) support `--agent-only` to hide manual issues, but there is no inverse `--manual` flag to show only manual issues within those same views.

There is a dedicated `dcat manual` subcommand, but it cannot be combined with the semantics of other listing subcommands (e.g. 'manual issues that are also in-review', 'manual issues that are blocked', 'manual issues ready to work').

Add a `--manual` option to every listing subcommand for symmetry with `--agent-only`. When set, only issues marked as manual are shown. `--manual` and `--agent-only` are mutually exclusive.

Scope — apply to these commands (the ones that list multiple issues):
- list
- ready
- blocked
- pr
- in-progress
- in-review
- deferred
- stale
- snoozed
- recently-added
- recently-closed
- open
- search

Helpers live in src/dogcat/cli/_helpers.py; the existing `--agent-only` wiring is a good reference for how to thread this through. Completers in src/dogcat/cli/_completions.py: `--manual` is a boolean flag so no completer is required, but verify tab completion still works for each affected command using tabcomp.py.
acceptance:
- Every listed subcommand accepts `--manual` and returns only issues where `manual=True`.
- `--manual` combined with `--agent-only` raises a clear error (mutually exclusive).
- `--manual` composes with other filters (--status, --type, --priority, --label, --namespace, etc.).
- Tests cover the flag on at least `list`, `ready`, and one of the status-specific commands (e.g. `in-progress`).
- `just lint-all` and `just test-all` pass.

Track metadata field changes in event log and dcat diff
description:
`dcat diff` and `dcat history` do not show changes to fields stored under `issue.metadata` (notably `manual` and `no_agent`). Flipping `--manual` on or off is invisible in the audit log and the diff output, even though it materially affects whether agents will pick up an issue.

## Root cause

`TRACKED_FIELDS` in `src/dogcat/constants.py:159` lists only top-level Issue fields. `metadata` is not included, so:

- `storage._tracked_changes()` (`src/dogcat/storage.py:469`) skips it when emitting events to the event log
- `_cmd_diff.py` (`src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_diff.py:253` and `:330`) only iterates `TRACKED_FIELDS`/`TRACKED_PROPOSAL_FIELDS`

## Proposed fix

Track metadata changes generally rather than special-casing `manual`. Diff the metadata dict and emit per-key changes (e.g. `metadata.manual`, `metadata.no_agent`) so any future metadata flag is automatically covered.

Touch points:
- `src/dogcat/storage.py` `_tracked_changes()` — also compare metadata key-by-key and emit nested changes
- `src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_diff.py` — same comparison logic against old/new states from git
- `src/dogcat/cli/_formatting.py` `format_event()` — render `metadata.<key>` changes readably (verify it does the right thing)

## Acceptance

- Toggling `dcat update <id> --manual` and `--no-manual` (or whatever the inverse is) appears in `dcat diff` and `dcat history`
- Same coverage for `no_agent` if/when it is set
- New tests covering metadata change detection in both the event log and the diff command
acceptance:
- `dcat diff` shows manual flag flips between HEAD and working tree
- `dcat history` records manual/no_agent metadata changes as events
- Tests cover both directions (flip on, flip off) for the storage event emission and the diff command

- `dcat history` records manual/no_agent metadata changes as events
- Tests cover both directions (flip on, flip off) for the storage event emission and the diff command

title: Track metadata field changes in event log and dcat diff
dcat label add/remove emits no event due to in-place list mutation
description:
`dcat label <id> add/remove` does not produce an event in `dcat history` or appear in `dcat diff`, even though the label change is persisted to storage.

## Reproduction

```
dcat create 'x'
dcat label dcat-xxx add -l foo
dcat history   # only the 'created' event — no labels change
```

By contrast, `dcat update --labels foo` works correctly and emits a `labels: [] -> ['foo']` event.

## Root cause

`src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_label.py:70-75` mutates `issue.labels` in place before calling `storage.update()`:

```python
if label_name not in issue.labels:
    issue.labels.append(label_name)            # in-place mutation
    updates: dict[str, Any] = {"labels": issue.labels}
    storage.update(issue_id, updates)
```

Inside `storage.update()` (`src/dogcat/storage.py:719`), the snapshot pattern is:

```python
old_values = {k: getattr(issue, k, None) for k in updates if k in TRACKED_FIELDS}
# ... mutate ...
new_values = {k: getattr(issue, k, None) for k in old_values}
```

Both snapshots take the same already-mutated list reference, so `_tracked_changes()` sees `old == new` and `_emit_event()` returns early on the empty changes dict.

The same shape applies to any list/dict-valued tracked field updated by reference rather than replaced.

## Proposed fix

Defensively copy list/dict values in `storage.update()`'s old-values snapshot so in-place mutations on the live Issue can't defeat the diff. One line in `_tracked_changes()` or at the snapshot site:

```python
def _snapshot(value):
    if isinstance(value, list):
        return list(value)
    if isinstance(value, dict):
        return dict(value)
    return value

old_values = {k: _snapshot(getattr(issue, k, None)) ...}
```

This is preferable to fixing each call site because it makes storage robust against the mistake.

## Acceptance

- `dcat label add/remove` emits an `updated` event with a `labels` change in `dcat history` and `dcat diff`
- `dcat update --labels ...` continues to emit events as before
- Test covering the in-place-mutation case for storage.update() (a list-valued tracked field mutated and passed back, asserting old != new in the emitted event)
acceptance:
- `dcat label <id> add`/`remove` produces a `labels: [...] -> [...]` event visible in `dcat history` and `dcat diff`
- `dcat update --labels` continues to work correctly
- Test asserting that mutating a list field in place and passing it through storage.update() still emits a correct old/new diff

title: dcat label add/remove emits no event due to in-place list mutation
dcat label add/remove emits no event due to in-place list mutation
description:
`dcat label <id> add/remove` does not produce an event in `dcat history` or appear in `dcat diff`, even though the label change is persisted to storage.

## Reproduction

```
dcat create 'x'
dcat label dcat-xxx add -l foo
dcat history   # only the 'created' event — no labels change
```

By contrast, `dcat update --labels foo` works correctly and emits a `labels: [] -> ['foo']` event.

## Root cause

`src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_label.py:70-75` mutates `issue.labels` in place before calling `storage.update()`:

```python
if label_name not in issue.labels:
    issue.labels.append(label_name)            # in-place mutation
    updates: dict[str, Any] = {"labels": issue.labels}
    storage.update(issue_id, updates)
```

Inside `storage.update()` (`src/dogcat/storage.py:719`), the snapshot pattern is:

```python
old_values = {k: getattr(issue, k, None) for k in updates if k in TRACKED_FIELDS}
# ... mutate ...
new_values = {k: getattr(issue, k, None) for k in old_values}
```

Both snapshots take the same already-mutated list reference, so `_tracked_changes()` sees `old == new` and `_emit_event()` returns early on the empty changes dict.

The same shape applies to any list/dict-valued tracked field updated by reference rather than replaced.

## Proposed fix

Defensively copy list/dict values in `storage.update()`'s old-values snapshot so in-place mutations on the live Issue can't defeat the diff. One line in `_tracked_changes()` or at the snapshot site:

```python
def _snapshot(value):
    if isinstance(value, list):
        return list(value)
    if isinstance(value, dict):
        return dict(value)
    return value

old_values = {k: _snapshot(getattr(issue, k, None)) ...}
```

This is preferable to fixing each call site because it makes storage robust against the mistake.

## Acceptance

- `dcat label add/remove` emits an `updated` event with a `labels` change in `dcat history` and `dcat diff`
- `dcat update --labels ...` continues to emit events as before
- Test covering the in-place-mutation case for storage.update() (a list-valued tracked field mutated and passed back, asserting old != new in the emitted event)
acceptance:
- `dcat label <id> add`/`remove` produces a `labels: [...] -> [...]` event visible in `dcat history` and `dcat diff`
- `dcat update --labels` continues to work correctly
- Test asserting that mutating a list field in place and passing it through storage.update() still emits a correct old/new diff

Add --has-comments / --without-comments filter to list commands
description:
Add a pair of mutually-exclusive boolean filters to all list-style commands so users can quickly find issues that have (or lack) comments.

## Flags
- `--has-comments` — show only issues with at least one comment
- `--without-comments` — show only issues with zero comments
- The two are mutually exclusive (raise typer.BadParameter if both are passed, similar to check_agent_manual_exclusive in src/dogcat/cli/_helpers.py)

## Comment semantics
Comments are hard-deleted from `issue.comments` (see src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_comment.py:130). There is no soft-delete on the Comment dataclass, so 'non-deleted comments' is just `bool(issue.comments)`. If a soft-delete is added later, this filter will need to be updated to skip tombstoned comments.

## Implementation
Add the filtering logic to `apply_common_filters` in src/dogcat/cli/_helpers.py (around line 256), alongside the existing agent_only/manual_only filters. Then thread two new `typer.Option` parameters through every list-style command:

- `dcat list` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py)
- `dcat ready` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py)
- `dcat blocked` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py)
- `dcat pr` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py)
- `dcat snoozed` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py)
- `dcat search` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_search.py)
- `dcat stale` (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_stale.py)
- Any other commands in _cmd_workflow.py that already accept --agent-only (audit by searching for 'agent_only: bool = typer.Option')

Inbox proposals (`dcat inbox list`) do not have comments and should be skipped.

## Acceptance
- Both flags work on every command listed above
- Passing both flags errors out with a clear message
- Tab completion is unaffected (these are boolean flags, no value completion needed)
- Tests cover: has-comments matches issues with comments, without-comments matches issues without, mutual-exclusion error, and combination with other filters (e.g. --agent-only --has-comments)
acceptance:
- --has-comments and --without-comments flags added to all list-style commands (list, ready, blocked, pr, snoozed, search, stale, plus any other --agent-only-bearing commands)
- Filter logic implemented once in apply_common_filters
- Mutual exclusion enforced with a clear error
- Tests added for both flags including combined-with-other-filter cases
- just lint-all passes

Add error handling for lock file open() across storage/inbox/event_log
description: Three file lock implementations call .open('w') without error handling. If the directory is missing or unwritable, the CLI crashes with an unhelpful OSError traceback. Locations: storage.py:228, inbox.py:106, event_log.py:188. Wrap each in try/except OSError with a descriptive RuntimeError.
acceptance: Lock file open failures produce a clear error message. Tests cover the OSError path.

title: Add error handling for lock file open() across storage/inbox/event_log

title: Add error handling for lock file open() across storage/inbox/event_log
Fix N+1 lookup patterns in deps.py
description: Five functions in deps.py repeatedly call storage.get() and storage.get_dependencies() per issue inside loops/recursion, causing O(n*d) performance. Affected: _has_deferred_ancestor (line 49-60), get_ready_work blocker check (71-88), get_blocked_issues (108-123), detect_cycles DFS (152-180), would_create_cycle (232-242). Fix: pre-build lookup dicts (all_issues_by_id, dep_graph) once before loops; memoize recursive calls.
acceptance: All deps.py functions pre-build lookup structures. No storage.get() calls inside loops. Existing tests still pass. Performance improvement measurable with large issue sets.

title: Fix N+1 lookup patterns in deps.py

Fix: pre-build lookup dicts (all_issues_by_id, dep_graph) once before loops; memoize recursive calls.
acceptance: All deps.py functions pre-build lookup structures. No storage.get() calls inside loops. Existing tests still pass. Performance improvement measurable with large issue sets.

title: Fix N+1 lookup patterns in deps.py
Add tests for storage.update() status transition edge cases
description: Only happy path tested for status transitions. Recent bug dogcat-36bt showed closed_at corruption on complex transitions. Missing test cases: (1) CLOSED->OPEN->CLOSED round-trip verifying closed_at/close_reason clear and reset correctly, (2) update status to CLOSED without closed_by, (3) CLOSED->BLOCKED->CLOSED verifying closed_at restored, (4) bulk status updates consistency.
acceptance: New tests in test_storage.py covering all four edge cases above. All tests pass.

Add warning logs for malformed records in merge conflict sections
description:
parse_conflicted_jsonl() in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py uses contextlib.suppress(orjson.JSONDecodeError) with no logging at lines 81 and 96 inside conflict sections. Data can be silently lost during merge with zero trace.

The non-conflict parser _parse_jsonl() already logs warnings for malformed lines (line 129-134) — this should be consistent. Malformed records in conflict sections should produce at least a warning log.

Files: src/dogcat/merge_driver.py:81,96 (conflict sections) vs :129-134 (existing warning pattern)

Add exhaustiveness check for unknown record types in classify_record()
description:
classify_record() in src/dogcat/models.py defaults to 'issue' for any unrecognized record_type value. If a new record type is added in the future without updating the merge driver, those records get misclassified and merged with wrong semantics — potentially causing silent data loss during merges.

Fix: Add an exhaustiveness check that warns or raises on unknown record types instead of silently defaulting to 'issue'. The merge driver (src/dogcat/merge_driver.py) relies on classify_record() at lines 207, 223, 251, 271, 297.

Files: src/dogcat/models.py:468-482, src/dogcat/merge_driver.py

description: Five functions in deps.py repeatedly call storage.get() and storage.get_dependencies() per issue inside loops/recursion, causing O(n*d) performance. Fix: pre-build lookup dicts (all_issues_by_id, dep_graph) once before loops; memoize recursive calls.
acceptance: All deps.py functions pre-build lookup structures. No storage.get() calls inside loops. Existing tests still pass. Performance improvement measurable with large issue sets.
closed_reason: Pre-built lookup dicts in deps.py to remove N+1 patterns

title: Code health sweep (March 2026)
Fix compaction race condition in storage layer
description:
_maybe_compact() in src/dogcat/storage.py checks line counts outside the lock (lines 417-428), then _save() acquires the lock separately. Two concurrent processes can both decide to compact redundantly.

More critically, _maybe_compact() is called after releasing the append lock (line 394), so line counts may be stale between the append completing and the compaction check running.

Fix: Move the compaction eligibility check inside the lock, or call _maybe_compact() within the append lock context.

title: Fix compaction race condition in storage layer
Rotate CSRF tokens per session instead of per app start
description:
The CSRF token in src/dogcat/web/propose/__init__.py is generated once at startup via secrets.token_urlsafe(32) and shared across all sessions. Any client that obtains it can reuse it indefinitely until server restart.

Fix: Rotate CSRF tokens per session or add expiry.

Apply DRY review findings: consolidate duplication and simplify wrong abstractions
description:
Umbrella epic for follow-ups from the should-i-abstract review (2026-04-25).

Three categories of findings:
- Abstract This: 10 instances of true knowledge duplication where the same algorithm or invariant lives in multiple places.
- Inline This: 3 wrong abstractions where the cost of indirection has grown past the cost of duplication.
- Leave Alone: 7 instances of incidental duplication that should stay separate (documented for future readers, no action).

Sub-issues are scoped to small, independently-shippable units. Pick them up in priority order (P1 first), or batch related ones (e.g. items 5/6/7 all touch the storage/inbox parallelism).

Consolidate hidden --all-namespaces / --namespace shim parameters
description:
The same 13-line hidden parameter declaration block is copied across ~10 commands purely to avoid breaking shell completion / global flag passing. 40 noqa: ARG001 markers across the CLI today.

Sites:
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_close.py:87-98, :170-181, :225-236
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_reopen.py:66-77
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_dep.py:49-60, :153-164
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_comment.py:37-48
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_label.py:41-52
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:636-649, :754-767, :808-819
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1218-1229, :1367-1378, :1888-1899, :1944-1955

Framework test: Test 1 — these change for the same reason (rename a flag, all 10+ copies move together).

Possible approaches:
1. Define HIDDEN_NS_PARAMS as a list of inspect.Parameter objects and splice into command signatures.
2. Wrap with _make_alias-style decorator that injects the params.
3. Move shim handling to the global Typer callback so commands don't need to declare them at all.

Option 3 is the cleanest if Typer allows accepting unknown options globally — investigate first.

Extract shared JSONLAppendStore base for storage and inbox
description:
JSONLStorage._save_locked (storage.py:282-383) and InboxStorage._save (inbox.py:110-138) implement the same atomic-rewrite-via-tempfile pattern: tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=self.dogcats_dir, suffix='.jsonl') -> write records -> fsync -> tmp_path.replace(self.path) with identical error wrapping.

Both classes also share:
- _needs_compaction flag
- _lock_path = self.dogcats_dir / '.issues.lock'
- _append() shape (with the trailing-newline check)
- _load() shape (with malformed-last-line tolerance)

Framework test: Test 1 (true duplication) + Test 3 (recurring durability pattern). Recent commits 1368fcb and dcfe702 show this code does change — and any safety improvement (e.g. directory-fsync after rename) must be replicated in both.

Action: Hoist a JSONLAppendStore base class (or at minimum a free-standing _atomic_rewrite_jsonl(path, records, dir) helper). Keep storage-specific concerns (event preservation, tombstone pruning, indexes) in JSONLStorage subclass.

Coordinates with dogcat-2my1 (compaction latency) — refactoring here unblocks performance work.

title.new: Extract shared JSONLAppendStore base for storage and inbox

description.new:
JSONLStorage._save_locked (storage.py:282-383) and InboxStorage._save (inbox.py:110-138) implement the same atomic-rewrite-via-tempfile pattern: tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=self.dogcats_dir, suffix='.jsonl') -> write records -> fsync -> tmp_path.replace(self.path) with identical error wrapping.

Both classes also share:
- _needs_compaction flag
- _lock_path = self.dogcats_dir / '.issues.lock'
- _append() shape (with the trailing-newline check)
- _load() shape (with malformed-last-line tolerance)

Framework test: Test 1 (true duplication) + Test 3 (recurring durability pattern). Recent commits 1368fcb and dcfe702 show this code does change — and any safety improvement (e.g. directory-fsync after rename) must be replicated in both.

Action: Hoist a JSONLAppendStore base class (or at minimum a free-standing _atomic_rewrite_jsonl(path, records, dir) helper). Keep storage-specific concerns (event preservation, tombstone pruning, indexes) in JSONLStorage subclass.

Coordinates with dogcat-2my1 (compaction latency) — refactoring here unblocks performance work.

title: Extract shared JSONLAppendStore base for storage and inbox
Split is_json_output into pure is_json + explicit set_json (remove side effect)
description:
_json_state.is_json_output(local_flag=True) currently mutates module-global _global_json as a side effect — calling the getter is also the setter. This is why 6 commands need a magic 'is_json_output(json_output)  # sync local flag for echo_error' line:

- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_label.py:57
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1383
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_rename_namespace.py:40
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:655
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_config.py:177
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_diff.py:188

Framework test: Test 5 (conditional accumulation / wrong abstraction). The fact that callers need a comment-marked ritual to invoke it is the smell.

Action:
1. Split _json_state into set_json(value: bool) (explicit setter) and is_json() -> bool (pure getter, no args).
2. Each command that has a --json typer option should call set_json(json_output) at the top of its body before any echo_error/typer.echo path.
3. Delete the magic 'sync' lines and their comments.

Extract apply_to_each helper for per-issue-id loop wrappers
description:
7 near-identical wrapper functions exist in CLI commands, each with the same shape: try storage method -> JSON-or-text echo on success -> echo_error(f'<verb> {id}: {e}') and return True on failure. Callers all run 'for id in ids: has_errors = wrapper(...) or has_errors' and 'raise typer.Exit(1)' at end.

Sites:
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_close.py:23-48 (_close_one), :125-149 (_delete_one)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_reopen.py:21-45 (_reopen_one)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_update.py:318-415 (_update_one)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_inbox.py:345-375 (_close_one), :440-463 (_delete_one), :645-672 (_reject_one)

Framework test: Test 2 (rule of 3 — 7 instances) + Test 1 (change for the same reason: JSON output formatting consistency, error-message templating).

Action: Add to _helpers.py:

  def apply_to_each(
      ids: list[str],
      op: Callable[[str], None],
      verb: str,
      *,
      json_output: bool,
  ) -> int:  # returns exit code
      ...

Each command's wrapper collapses to a single 'op' closure that calls the storage method and echoes success.

Hoist resolve_id to shared resolve_partial_id helper
description:
JSONLStorage.resolve_id (storage.py:592-631) and InboxStorage.resolve_id (inbox.py:256-293) implement the same algorithm: exact match -> suffix match -> ambiguity error with same message format.

Subtle divergence (test for drift after refactor): storage uses split('-', 1)[-1] while inbox uses rsplit('-', 1)[-1]. The inbox can have multi-segment full IDs like 'dogcat-inbox-X' so rsplit is the correct choice; storage's split is a latent bug for any namespace containing a hyphen.

Framework test: Test 1 (same algorithm) + the existing divergence is itself evidence the duplication has drifted.

Action:
1. Create dogcat/_id_resolve.py with: resolve_partial_id(partial: str, ids: Iterable[str]) -> str | None
2. Use rsplit (the correct version) and add a test for hyphenated namespaces.
3. Replace both call sites with a thin wrapper.

Hoist _tracked_changes and _field_value into shared diff helper
description:
Three places define the same enum-normalize-and-diff pattern:

- src/dogcat/storage.py:490-511 (_field_value, _tracked_changes)
- src/dogcat/inbox.py:200-216 (_tracked_changes)
- src/dogcat/cli/_validate.py:436-440 (_field_value)

Framework test: Test 1 — these change together when the enum normalization rule changes (or a new value-like type appears).

Action: Move to a new shared module dogcat/_diff.py:

  def field_value(value: Any) -> Any:
      return value.value if hasattr(value, 'value') else value

  def tracked_changes(
      old: dict[str, Any],
      new: dict[str, Any],
      tracked: frozenset[str],
  ) -> dict[str, dict[str, Any]]:
      ...

Both classes call into the shared function with their own TRACKED_FIELDS / TRACKED_PROPOSAL_FIELDS constant.

Push _emit_event into _BaseEventLog
description:
JSONLStorage._emit_event (storage.py:460-488) and InboxStorage._emit_event (inbox.py:170-198) both build an EventRecord and best-effort-append to the event log with a debug log on failure. Same shape.

_BaseEventLog already exists at event_log.py:82-146 and abstracts the file/lock layer. The emit-with-best-effort semantics belong there too.

Framework test: Test 1 + Test 3 (base class exists, just doesn't yet capture this pattern).

Action: Add _BaseEventLog.emit(event_type, full_id, updated_at_iso, by, title, changes) that constructs the EventRecord and wraps the append in the best-effort try/except logging block. Call sites in storage.py and inbox.py become one-liners.

Use apply_common_filters in holdout commands (recently_added, pr, list)
description:
_helpers.apply_common_filters (lines 287-353) already centralizes manual/agent/has-comments/namespace filters, but three commands reimplement the same filter loops inline:

- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1595-1606 (recently_added) — manual/agent_only + has_comments/without_comments
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1798-1824 (pr / progress_review) — same filters duplicated for ip_issues and ir_issues lists (4 copies of the manual predicate)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:386-402 (list) — same filters

Framework test: Test 3 — abstraction exists; bypassing it is how is_manual_issue inlining (sub-issue dogcat-30a6) keeps recurring.

Action: switch these call sites to apply_common_filters(...) with the right keyword args. The 'pr' command's two-list shape may need apply_common_filters called twice (once per status list) — that's fine.

Promote _resolve_dogcats_path and reuse instead of inline find_dogcats_dir walks
description:
The idiom 'if not Path(dogcats_dir).is_dir(): dogcats_dir = find_dogcats_dir()' is repeated 6 times in src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_inbox.py at lines 32-35, 42-50, 217-219, 244-246, 566-568, 703-706.

Meanwhile config.py:229-266 already defines _resolve_dogcats_path doing essentially the same walk-up (with extra .dogcatrc handling).

Framework test: Test 3 — abstraction exists; it's just hidden behind a leading underscore in config.py.

Action:
1. Decide the canonical name (resolve_dogcats_dir) and home (likely _helpers.py or a new _paths.py).
2. Promote/move the function (drop the leading underscore on the public surface).
3. Replace all 6 inline walks in _cmd_inbox.py.

Drop redundant check_*_exclusive calls (validation runs twice)
description:
_helpers.apply_common_filters validates agent/manual + has/without-comments exclusivity internally (lines 313-316). But several callers also validate the same pair before calling it:

- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:298-305 (list command)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1462 (recently_closed)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1577 (recently_added)
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1786-1787 (pr / progress_review)

The validation runs twice in those paths.

Framework test: Test 5 (conditional accumulation) — validators leaked into a transformation function, so callers re-validate defensively.

Action: pick one — either drop the calls from apply_common_filters (since validation isn't its job) or drop them from the callers. Recommendation: keep them in apply_common_filters (it's the common path) and remove the caller-side checks. Callers that don't use apply_common_filters (e.g. recently-closed) keep their explicit checks.

Add undo/rollback mechanism for agent mistakes
description: Event log records all changes but can't restore prior state. Agents that make mistakes (accidental close, wrong title change) have no recovery beyond dcat reopen. Consider a dcat undo command that replays event log to a previous state.

title: Add undo/rollback mechanism for agent mistakes
Back the 'works fine with git merges and rebases' claim with a thorough test suite
description:
We claim dogcat works fine with git merges, rebases, cherry-picks, etc. Today the claim rests on a hand-picked set of scenarios in tests/test_merge.py, tests/test_merge_driver.py, and tests/test_git_rebase.py. That coverage is decent but ad-hoc — it does not span the matrix of git workflows users actually do, nor does it use property-based / generative testing to find the cases we did not think of.

Goal: build a test suite comprehensive enough that we can credibly say 'this works'. It is fine if the suite reveals problems — that is the point. Each child task adds a focused slice of coverage (or doc).

Scope is intentionally broad. Children can land independently; later children may depend on earlier ones (e.g. property tests may reveal bugs that need fixing before workflow matrix tests pass). Use 'in_review' carefully — if a test file demonstrates a real bug, file the bug separately rather than skipping the test.

This epic is BLOCKED by other in-progress work — do not start until cleared.
acceptance: All child tasks closed or have a clear follow-up issue covering any failing tests. The merge/rebase coverage matrix doc (final child) lists every claim we make in merge_driver.py module docstring and ties it to at least one test.

acceptance.new: All child tasks closed or have a clear follow-up issue covering any failing tests. The merge/rebase coverage matrix doc (final child) lists every claim we make in merge_driver.py module docstring and ties it to at least one test.

Property-based merge invariant tests with hypothesis
description:
Add a hypothesis-based test module that generates random sequences of issue/proposal/dep/link/event mutations on two branches and asserts the invariants documented in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring.

Invariants to verify:
- **Idempotency**: merge_jsonl(R, R, R) == R (record set equality)
- **Convergence / eventual consistency**: starting from a common base, applying disjoint sequences of mutations on ours/theirs and merging in EITHER order produces semantically equal record sets
- **Monotonicity in updated_at** for issues: a record with a later updated_at on either side is never replaced by an older one
- **Finality monotonicity for proposals**: tombstone > closed > open is absorbing — once one side reaches a higher rank, the merge result keeps it
- **Deletion wins over silence**: a remove on one side (and no observation on the other) survives the merge
- **Re-add wins over a stale delete**: an add by side A after a delete observed in base by side B is preserved

Where to put it: new file tests/test_merge_properties.py. Use hypothesis strategies to build records (the helpers in tests/test_merge_driver.py already define _issue_record/_event_record/_proposal_record/_dep_record — reuse and extend).

Acceptance: at least one hypothesis test per invariant above, each with @given strategies that explore non-trivial cases (multi-record sets, mixed kinds, both add and remove ops). Failing inputs should be shrinkable. If any invariant fails for a generated case, file a separate bug — do NOT mark the test xfail without a linked issue.
acceptance: tests/test_merge_properties.py exists, runs under just test-all, covers all six invariants, and any discovered failures have linked bug issues.

title.new: Property-based merge invariant tests with hypothesis

acceptance.new: tests/test_merge_properties.py exists, runs under just test-all, covers all six invariants, and any discovered failures have linked bug issues.

Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert
description:
Today tests/test_merge.py and tests/test_merge_driver.py only exercise the standard 'git merge feature into main' flow. Users do other things git supports — and we have not verified the merge driver behaves correctly under them.

Scenarios to add (one test class each, in tests/test_git_workflows.py — new file, alongside existing test_git_rebase.py):

1. **Cherry-pick**: branch A has commits creating issue X and editing issue Y. From main, cherry-pick the X-create commit only. Verify the resulting JSONL is valid, issue X exists, no stray markers, no events lost.

2. **Octopus merge**: three branches each create a different issue. `git merge branch1 branch2 branch3` (or sequential 3-way) — verify all three issues land, deps/links from any branch survive, no conflict markers.

3. **Squash merge**: make 5 commits on a feature branch (create issue, comment, label, snooze, close). `git merge --squash` and commit. Verify final state matches what a fast-forward would have produced.

4. **Revert merge commit**: merge feature, then `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>`. Verify the JSONL state correctly reverts to pre-merge — and that re-merging the feature branch afterward still works (this is a known git footgun).

Use the existing GitRepo fixture from tests/conftest.py. Mirror the style of tests/test_merge.py:_create_issue_on_branch helpers.

If any scenario reveals a real bug in the merge driver, file a separate bug issue with a minimal repro and do not skip — the test should fail loudly so we know.
acceptance: tests/test_git_workflows.py covers all four scenarios. Each test ends by verifying (a) no conflict markers in .dogcats/issues.jsonl, (b) JSONLStorage loads cleanly, (c) expected issue/event state matches what the workflow should have produced.

title.new: Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert

Today tests/test_merge.py and tests/test_merge_driver.py only exercise the standard 'git merge feature into main' flow. Users do other things git supports — and we have not verified the merge driver behaves correctly under them.

Scenarios to add (one test class each, in tests/test_git_workflows.py — new file, alongside existing test_git_rebase.py):

1. **Cherry-pick**: branch A has commits creating issue X and editing issue Y. From main, cherry-pick the X-create commit only. Verify the resulting JSONL is valid, issue X exists, no stray markers, no events lost.

2. **Octopus merge**: three branches each create a different issue. `git merge branch1 branch2 branch3` (or sequential 3-way) — verify all three issues land, deps/links from any branch survive, no conflict markers.

3. **Squash merge**: make 5 commits on a feature branch (create issue, comment, label, snooze, close). `git merge --squash` and commit. Verify final state matches what a fast-forward would have produced.

4. **Revert merge commit**: merge feature, then `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>`. Verify the JSONL state correctly reverts to pre-merge — and that re-merging the feature branch afterward still works (this is a known git footgun).

Use the existing GitRepo fixture from tests/conftest.py. Mirror the style of tests/test_merge.py:_create_issue_on_branch helpers.

If any scenario reveals a real bug in the merge driver, file a separate bug issue with a minimal repro and do not skip — the test should fail loudly so we know.

acceptance.new: tests/test_git_workflows.py covers all four scenarios. Each test ends by verifying (a) no conflict markers in .dogcats/issues.jsonl, (b) JSONLStorage loads cleanly, (c) expected issue/event state matches what the workflow should have produced.

title: Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert
Pull/rebase variant matrix tests
description:
Users invoke our merge driver via several distinct git operations beyond plain 'git merge'. Each path is a different invocation of the merge machinery and is worth testing separately:

- `git pull` (default — merge)
- `git pull --rebase` (rebase, our existing test_git_rebase.py covers some of this — extend, do not duplicate)
- `git pull --ff-only` (should never invoke the driver; verify and assert clean)
- `git fetch && git merge origin/main` (explicit form)
- `git rebase --interactive` with a reorder (different from straight rebase — multiple driver invocations)

Where: tests/test_pull_variants.py (new file). Build a 'remote' repo via a bare repo fixture (or a sibling directory with file:// URL) so pull operations are real, not faked.

For each variant, run a scenario where:
- both sides have created at least one issue not on the other side
- both sides have edited the same issue (latest updated_at should win)

Verify: post-operation file is valid JSONL, no markers, no records lost, expected LWW result for the contested edit.

Acceptance criterion is the matrix itself — every row covered, every assertion explicit. If --ff-only fails because of pre-existing config, document why and skip that one row with a clear reason.
acceptance: tests/test_pull_variants.py covers all five rows. Each test verifies post-operation file integrity and the LWW outcome for the contested edit.

acceptance.new: tests/test_pull_variants.py covers all five rows. Each test verifies post-operation file integrity and the LWW outcome for the contested edit.

description:
The is_manual_issue(metadata) helper exists in models.py:28-34 but is bypassed by inlined 'metadata.get("manual") or metadata.get("no_agent")' at 6+ call sites:

- src/dogcat/cli/_formatting.py:194, :559
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:390, :396
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_workflow.py:1311, :1599-1600, :1605, :1801, :1812, :1817
- src/dogcat/tui/shared.py
- src/dogcat/tui/detail_panel.py

Framework test: Test 3 (existing abstraction) + Test 1 (true business-rule duplication, two-flag exception). A future addition like metadata.get('ai_only') would have to be hunted across 10+ sites.

Action: replace each inline expression with is_manual_issue(issue.metadata).

title: Use is_manual_issue() helper everywhere instead of inlined metadata flag check
Long-divergence and scale stress tests for merge driver
description:
Existing merge tests use small fixtures (typically 1-3 records per side). Real users may diverge for weeks with hundreds of mutations on both sides. We have no tests for that.

Add tests/test_merge_scale.py with:

1. **Wide divergence**: 100 issues created on each side (no overlap). After merge, assert all 200 are present and JSONLStorage loads in <1s. Measures correctness AND performance.

2. **Heavy contention**: same set of 50 issues edited 5 times each on both sides with interleaved updated_at values. Verify LWW picks the absolute latest per issue.

3. **Mixed-type churn**: 50 issues, 100 deps (some added then removed), 50 links, 200 events on each side. Verify merge result has correct dep/link replay state and event union.

4. **Compaction-mixed**: one side has run compaction (rewriting the file as a single snapshot), the other side has 100 raw event-log records on top of base. Merge should reconcile — this is the realistic 'one dev compacted on main, you've been on a feature branch' case.

Use pytest's tmp_path. Optionally use pytest-benchmark for the perf assertions, but plain time.perf_counter() with a generous bound (say 5s) is fine — we want to catch O(n²) regressions, not measure exact ns.

Cross-reference: dogcat-5tdx (formalize benchmark suite) is broader; this issue is specifically merge-driver scale, not general benchmarking.
acceptance: tests/test_merge_scale.py runs under just test-all in <30s total. Each scenario verifies functional correctness AND records a soft perf bound.

Multi-developer workflow simulation tests
description:
Existing merge tests are unit-style: each one constructs a specific scenario. Users hit problems in *combinations* — Alice creates and labels, Bob comments and snoozes, Alice merges Bob's branch, Bob rebases on top of Alice's main, etc.

Add tests/test_multidev_workflows.py simulating realistic days-of-work for 2-3 developers. Each test scripts a sequence of dcat CLI invocations on separate branches, intersperses pulls/merges/rebases, and asserts the final state matches what every developer would see if they all sync'd.

Concrete scenarios (one test method each):

1. **Pair workflow**: Alice creates issue A, branches off; Bob on main creates issue B, comments on A. Alice's branch labels A and pushes. Bob pulls. Final: Bob sees A with Alice's labels AND his comment.

2. **Triangle**: Three devs, each creates two issues and edits one of the others' issues. They merge in sequence (1→2→3→1). Final state: all six issues, all three edits applied with LWW semantics.

3. **Late-comer rebase**: Alice has been on a feature branch for 'a week' (5 commits, 10 mutations). Main has had 20 mutations from others. Alice rebases. Verify file integrity at every step of the rebase and final state correctness.

4. **Cross-merge**: Alice and Bob each merge each other's branches into their own (non-symmetric — Alice merges Bob first, Bob merges Alice second). Verify both end up at the same state (eventual consistency on the operator level).

Build helpers in tests/_multidev.py that wrap 'become Alice' / 'become Bob' (toggle git config user.name/email, switch branches). Use the GitRepo fixture as the shared repo.
acceptance: tests/test_multidev_workflows.py has all four scenarios passing OR has linked bug issues for failures. Final-state assertions compare the loaded JSONLStorage on each developer's view, not just file contents.

Concurrent compaction race tests
description:
Compaction rewrites .dogcats/issues.jsonl into a snapshot of effective state. If both sides run compaction independently and then merge, the result must still be correct — but our existing tests only cover one-side compaction (test_merge.py:test_one_side_compaction_auto_resolves and test_both_sides_compaction_conflict).

What's missing:

1. **Identical-content compaction**: both sides compact at the same logical state (same set of effective records) but the resulting JSONL byte order differs. The merge should produce a clean result without spurious LWW shuffling.

2. **Compact-then-edit-on-both**: both sides compact, then both make additional edits to different issues. Merge should preserve all post-compaction edits AND correctly LWW any contested edits.

3. **Compact-then-edit-on-one-only**: one side compacts and makes edits; the other side's branch never compacted but has overlapping edits. This is the typical 'main was compacted last week, my branch was not' scenario.

4. **Compact-with-remove-ops**: side A compacts (which materializes effective state and drops add+remove pairs from history); side B has the original add records on a feature branch. Merge must not resurrect the removed records.

Add as a new test class TestConcurrentCompaction in tests/test_merge.py (alongside the existing one-side test) or in a new file tests/test_compaction_merge.py if the class grows large.

Use the _force_compaction helper already defined in tests/test_merge.py.
acceptance: All four scenarios covered. Scenario 4 in particular asserts the compaction-snapshot's state is preserved and not overridden by feature-branch raw add records.

Manual conflict resolution recovery + doctor detection tests
description:
If a user does not have the merge driver installed, or has it installed but git falls back to plain text merge for some reason (e.g. detached HEAD with no .gitattributes scope), they may end up resolving conflicts by hand. Today our doctor command (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_doctor.py) catches some of this — but we don't have explicit tests for the recovery flow.

Tests to add (tests/test_manual_conflict_recovery.py):

1. **User accepts 'ours' wholesale** (drops theirs side): doctor should detect the missing records via event log replay (events from both sides exist; some issues referenced by events are absent).

2. **User accepts 'theirs' wholesale**: symmetric — doctor detects.

3. **User leaves conflict markers in the file**: parse_conflicted_jsonl should warn (already covered by test_merge_driver.py:test_conflict_markers_log_warning) — but doctor should also surface this when run in this state.

4. **User produces invalid JSONL during manual edit** (typo, partial line): doctor should report the bad line numbers without crashing.

5. **Recovery via dcat git auto-resolve**: take a manually-broken file, run dcat git auto-resolve (verify command exists; if not, link the missing-feature issue), confirm it reconstructs valid state.

Reference: src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_doctor.py for current detector logic. The post-merge check (test_merge_driver.py:test_doctor_post_merge_detects_concurrent_edits) is a starting point.

Acceptance: each scenario has an explicit test asserting (a) what doctor reports and (b) the suggested fix command (if any) actually works.
acceptance: tests/test_manual_conflict_recovery.py covers all five scenarios. Doctor outputs are asserted on (not just exit codes) so future doctor changes do not silently regress messaging.

acceptance.new: tests/test_manual_conflict_recovery.py covers all five scenarios. Doctor outputs are asserted on (not just exit codes) so future doctor changes do not silently regress messaging.

Edge-case file states in merge: unrelated histories, empty, only-shared
description:
Edge cases that current tests do not cover:

1. **git merge --allow-unrelated-histories**: two repos initialized independently, both with .dogcats/issues.jsonl, then joined. The merge driver receives no common base. Verify it falls through to a sensible 'union of both' result.

2. **Empty file on one side**: ours is empty, theirs has records (or vice versa). Should produce theirs' content with no warnings.

3. **Empty file on both sides**: trivially empty merged file, no spurious warnings.

4. **File-only-in-base, deleted-on-both**: edge case for file rename detection / deletion.

5. **Trailing whitespace / missing final newline**: shouldn't matter, but verify the merged output is canonical (orjson + newlines, no trailing whitespace).

6. **Records with unknown record types** (forward-compat): both sides have records that classify_record() returns None for. Verify they pass through unchanged rather than being dropped — important for forward-compatibility with future schema additions.

Add as TestEdgeCaseFileStates in tests/test_merge_driver.py (extend existing TestMergeJSONL or sibling class).

Note: case 6 may currently DROP unknown records — if so, that is a real bug. File a follow-up issue and keep the test as a failing assertion until fixed (do not xfail without an issue).
acceptance: All six edge cases covered. Case 6 in particular has either a passing test or a linked bug issue if it currently fails.
