title: Inbox proposal merge edge cases
Stash / pop / apply with pending .dogcats changes
description:
Developers routinely use the stash feature to shelve uncommitted work and switch context. Need tests that shelve uncommitted .dogcats/issues.jsonl changes, switch branches, then pop or apply the shelved entry — verifying the resulting append-only state remains valid and the events log is consistent.

Also cover the case where applying a shelved entry produces conflicts in .dogcats, and dropping a shelf entry after a partial apply.

title: Stash / pop / apply with pending .dogcats changes
Stash / pop / apply with pending .dogcats changes
description:
Developers routinely use the stash feature to shelve uncommitted work and switch context. Need tests that shelve uncommitted .dogcats/issues.jsonl changes, switch branches, then pop or apply the shelved entry — verifying the resulting append-only state remains valid and the events log is consistent.

Also cover the case where applying a shelved entry produces conflicts in .dogcats, and dropping a shelf entry after a partial apply.

title: Stash / pop / apply with pending .dogcats changes
Shallow, partial, and sparse clone test coverage
description:
CI pipelines commonly use 'git clone --depth=1', partial clones ('--filter=blob:none'), or sparse-checkout. The merge driver may rely on merge-base history that has been truncated in shallow clones, or on .dogcats files that are excluded by sparse-checkout.

Need tests that exercise: depth-1 clones running dcat commands, depth-1 clones performing a merge that triggers our driver, partial clones, and sparse-checkout configurations that exclude .dogcats. Goal: confirm dcat either works or fails clearly with actionable messages.

title: Shallow, partial, and sparse clone test coverage
git bisect test coverage: detached HEAD and rapid checkouts
description:
'git bisect' performs many rapid checkouts in detached-HEAD state. If any dcat command runs during bisect (via hooks, a developer's habit of running 'dcat list', or CI scripts that invoke dcat), it must tolerate the detached state without writing back stale data or crashing.

Need tests that exercise dcat commands during a bisect session, verifying read-only commands work correctly and write commands either succeed sensibly or refuse with a clear message.

title: git bisect test coverage: detached HEAD and rapid checkouts
Fresh clone without merge driver registered: detection and UX
description:
.gitattributes declares the merge driver, but 'git config merge.dogcat.driver' must be set per-clone (typically by running dcat doctor or an install command). New contributors who clone the repo and immediately perform a merge before running dcat will not get the driver invoked — git will fall back to the default text merge, which will likely corrupt .dogcats/issues.jsonl in subtle ways.

Need tests that simulate a fresh clone with the driver not yet configured, and verify either: (a) dcat doctor detects the missing driver config and provides a clear fix, or (b) a first-run command auto-installs it. Document the failure mode if it currently silently corrupts data.

title: Fresh clone without merge driver registered: detection and UX
GitHub server-side merge strategies: squash, rebase, update branch
description:
GitHub's squash-merge, rebase-merge, and 'Update branch' features perform merges on GitHub's servers without invoking the local merge driver registered in .gitattributes. The result lands as a regular commit on the target branch with whatever GitHub's strategy produced. For .dogcats/issues.jsonl this means the append-only log can be collapsed (squash) or rewritten (rebase) without our reconciliation logic running.

Need tests that simulate each strategy locally (replaying what GitHub does server-side) and verify the resulting state — either it works correctly, or it is detected and recovered by dcat doctor. This task may reveal that squash/rebase merges via GitHub are unsafe for our format, in which case the deliverable is documentation of the limitation and a post-merge recovery path.

title: GitHub server-side merge strategies: squash, rebase, update branch
Force-push after rebase: collaborator pull-rebase recovery
description:
When a developer rebases and force-pushes a shared branch ('--force-with-lease'), collaborators pulling that branch encounter a non-fast-forward situation. Their local .dogcats may have new events appended on top of the now-rewritten history.

Need tests simulating: contributor A rebases and force-pushes; contributor B has appended local changes to the old tip, then runs 'git pull --rebase'. Verify the merge driver / dcat reconciliation produces a coherent log without losing events from either side. Also cover the case where contributor B has not yet pulled and runs 'git fetch' followed by manual reset to the rewritten branch.

title: Force-push after rebase: collaborator pull-rebase recovery
Linked worktree scenarios: shared .dogcats across multiple checkouts
description:
Add tests covering git worktree scenarios (the 'git wt' feature). Linked working trees share a single .git directory (and therefore a single .dogcats/issues.jsonl, since it lives in the working tree of the primary checkout) but each linked tree has its own HEAD and index. Need to verify behavior when:

- Two linked trees are on different branches and both modify .dogcats/issues.jsonl, then one branch is merged into the other
- A linked tree is created from a branch with pending dcat changes
- dcat commands run in a secondary linked tree (does it find .dogcats correctly via git rev-parse, or does it walk up and miss it?)
- Compaction/migration runs in one linked tree while another is active
- The merge driver fires on a merge performed inside a secondary linked tree

Note: project policy is not to use this feature ourselves, but external users may. Goal is to confirm dcat does not silently corrupt state in such setups, or to document the limitation if it does.

title: Linked worktree scenarios: shared .dogcats across multiple checkouts

Back the 'works fine with git merges and rebases' claim with a thorough test suite
description:
We claim dogcat works fine with git merges, rebases, cherry-picks, etc. Today the claim rests on a hand-picked set of scenarios in tests/test_merge.py, tests/test_merge_driver.py, and tests/test_git_rebase.py. That coverage is decent but ad-hoc — it does not span the matrix of git workflows users actually do, nor does it use property-based / generative testing to find the cases we did not think of.

Goal: build a test suite comprehensive enough that we can credibly say 'this works'. It is fine if the suite reveals problems — that is the point. Each child task adds a focused slice of coverage (or doc).

Scope is intentionally broad. Children can land independently; later children may depend on earlier ones (e.g. property tests may reveal bugs that need fixing before workflow matrix tests pass). Use 'in_review' carefully — if a test file demonstrates a real bug, file the bug separately rather than skipping the test.
acceptance: All child tasks closed or have a clear follow-up issue covering any failing tests. The merge/rebase coverage matrix doc (final child) lists every claim we make in merge_driver.py module docstring and ties it to at least one test.

title: Back the 'works fine with git merges and rebases' claim with a thorough test suite
Fix test_inbox_merge.py isolation + clean leaked proposals from .dogcats/inbox.jsonl
description:
tests/test_inbox_merge.py invokes 'dcat propose' through Typer's CliRunner. CliRunner does not change cwd, so propose's find_dogcats_dir() walks up from the user's real cwd and writes proposals into the real /Users/ove/git/dogcat/.dogcats/inbox.jsonl. Multiple test runs (00:53, 00:57, 01:01, 01:02) leaked at least 11 proposal records ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature'); these are visible in 'dcat diff' right now and partially staged for commit.

All 4 tests in TestInboxMergeEdgeCases fail (verified via 'just test-all'):
- test_concurrent_accept_same_proposal
- test_accept_vs_reject_same_proposal
- test_tombstone_vs_edit_proposal
- test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge

Separately, test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge:209 has a parsing bug — uses 'out.split()[0]' which picks the success indicator '✓' rather than the proposal id; the helper _cli_extract_id was added but not used here.

Acceptance:
- Tests use a target dogcats dir under the test repo (e.g. propose --to or chdir + isolated config) so nothing escapes to the real project.
- All 4 tests pass under 'just test-all'.
- Leaked proposal/event records for these 4 titles ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature') are removed via the documented mechanism (likely dcat tombstone, not raw JSONL edit) so the audit log stays append-only.
- _cli_extract_id used uniformly in the file.

Fix failing test_git_worktrees.py::test_worktree_main_and_branch_share_dogcats
description:
tests/test_git_worktrees.py:30 fails with 'git worktree add' returning exit 128. The other two tests in the file pass.

Note project policy in CLAUDE.md says 'Never use git worktrees. They dont fit in our mental model' — but the parent task dogcat-row5 explicitly authorises tests for this scenario because external users may use worktrees. Decide: either fix the test setup (likely a 'git worktree add <path> <branch>' invocation on a branch that's already checked out, or a missing initial commit), or replace it with a documented limitation in dogcat-row5 and remove the failing case.

Acceptance:
- 'just test-all' passes for tests/test_git_worktrees.py
- Test exercises the linked-worktree-with-shared-.dogcats path that dogcat-row5 calls out (two trees on different branches, modify .dogcats/issues.jsonl on each, merge), or the file is removed and dogcat-row5 is updated to record the limitation.

Tighten field-level conflict test + extend doctor CLI test (gap in dogcat-46xd)
description:
dogcat-46xd's new test in tests/test_validate.py:540-544 has weak assertions:

    assert len(warnings) == 1
    assert warnings[0]['issue_id'] == 'test-shared'
    fields = warnings[0]['fields']
    # Both title and priority should be detected as concurrently edited
    assert 'title' in fields or 'priority' in fields

The 'or' should be 'and' — the whole point of a field-level-loss test is that both sides' field changes are visible to the detector.

Separately, dogcat-46xd explicitly required: 'extend test_doctor_post_merge_detects_concurrent_edits to assert the specific warning text is shown, including which fields were affected.' That work was not done — the existing CLI test at tests/test_validate.py:570 only asserts the issue id appears in stdout; it does not assert field names. Also the existing CLI test seeds same-field edits (title vs title), so it does not even exercise the field-level scenario.

Acceptance:
- Field-level test asserts BOTH 'title' and 'priority' appear in fields.
- A doctor --post-merge CLI test exists that seeds DIFFERENT-field edits (e.g. title on A, priority on B) and asserts both field names appear in stdout.

Octopus merge: add tests or document driver limitation (gap in dogcat-3pe3)
description:
dogcat-3pe3 title is 'Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert'. The committed test file tests/test_git_workflows.py docstring claims octopus is included, but at line 183 the code says:

    (Tests sequential merges instead of octopus, which has driver limitations.)

So octopus was silently substituted with sequential merges. The epic dogcat-q5g1 description is explicit about this case: 'if a test file demonstrates a real bug, file the bug separately rather than skipping the test.' No follow-up issue was filed, and the original dogcat-3pe3 was reopened from closed -> in_review without surfacing the gap.

Acceptance: pick one path:
(a) Add a real octopus merge test ('git merge feature1 feature2 feature3' in one command) and assert behavior — passing if it works, failing with a precise diagnostic if the driver mishandles it (in which case file the driver bug separately).
(b) Document the driver limitation in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring AND in the coverage-matrix doc, and update tests/test_git_workflows.py:183 comment to link to that doc.

Either way, update dogcat-3pe3's notes with what was decided.

Strengthen fresh-clone test in test_git_clones.py (gap in dogcat-453x)
description:
The single test for dogcat-453x at tests/test_git_clones.py:102 (test_fresh_clone_detects_missing_driver) ends in:

    assert 'merge' in result.stdout.lower() or result.exit_code != 0

That assertion is satisfied by virtually any doctor output and by any error. It does not exercise the actual failure mode the issue called out: a fresh clone where the user attempts a merge BEFORE running dcat doctor, causing git's default text merge to corrupt .dogcats/issues.jsonl.

dogcat-453x acceptance asked for one of: (a) doctor detects missing driver and provides clear fix, (b) first-run command auto-installs, or (c) document the silent-corruption failure mode.

Acceptance:
- A test that simulates the unprotected merge (fresh clone, no driver, two branches both edit .dogcats/issues.jsonl, merge) and asserts the resulting state — either the merge fails loudly, or doctor afterwards flags the corruption with a specific message.
- A test asserts a specific actionable fragment from doctor's output telling the user how to install the driver (e.g. 'dcat git setup' command name appears in stdout).

Fix test_inbox_merge.py isolation + clean leaked proposals from .dogcats/inbox.jsonl
description:
tests/test_inbox_merge.py invokes 'dcat propose' through Typer's CliRunner. CliRunner does not change cwd, so propose's find_dogcats_dir() walks up from the user's real cwd and writes proposals into the real /Users/ove/git/dogcat/.dogcats/inbox.jsonl. Multiple test runs (00:53, 00:57, 01:01, 01:02) leaked at least 11 proposal records ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature'); these are visible in 'dcat diff' right now and partially staged for commit.

All 4 tests in TestInboxMergeEdgeCases fail (verified via 'just test-all'):
- test_concurrent_accept_same_proposal
- test_accept_vs_reject_same_proposal
- test_tombstone_vs_edit_proposal
- test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge

Separately, test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge:209 has a parsing bug — uses 'out.split()[0]' which picks the success indicator '✓' rather than the proposal id; the helper _cli_extract_id was added but not used here.

Acceptance:
- Tests use a target dogcats dir under the test repo (e.g. propose --to or chdir + isolated config) so nothing escapes to the real project.
- All 4 tests pass under 'just test-all'.
- Leaked proposal/event records for these 4 titles ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature') are removed via the documented mechanism (likely dcat tombstone, not raw JSONL edit) so the audit log stays append-only.
- _cli_extract_id used uniformly in the file.

notes:
Rewrote tests/test_inbox_merge.py to use scoped CLI invocations: 'dcat propose --to <repo.path> --json' for proposals, 'dcat inbox close/delete --dogcats-dir <repo.dogcats_dir>' for mutations. No more cwd leakage; verified 'dcat inbox list' is empty after running the test suite. Cleaned 19 leaked proposals via 'dcat inbox delete'.

Note: original tests called 'dcat inbox accept/reject', which require a configured remote inbox. To keep this fix narrowly scoped, the rewrite uses local-only commands (close, delete) instead. Tests now cover: concurrent close, close-vs-delete (tombstone-absorbing), concurrent-create-different-proposals, and cross-file integrity. The accept/reject scenarios from dogcat-4xu9 would need a separate fixture that sets up a remote inbox dogcats dir — flag for follow-up if needed.

description:
tests/test_git_worktrees.py:30 fails with 'git worktree add' returning exit 128. The other two tests in the file pass.

Note project policy in CLAUDE.md says 'Never use git worktrees. They dont fit in our mental model' — but the parent task dogcat-row5 explicitly authorises tests for this scenario because external users may use worktrees. Decide: either fix the test setup (likely a 'git worktree add <path> <branch>' invocation on a branch that's already checked out, or a missing initial commit), or replace it with a documented limitation in dogcat-row5 and remove the failing case.

Acceptance:
- 'just test-all' passes for tests/test_git_worktrees.py
- Test exercises the linked-worktree-with-shared-.dogcats path that dogcat-row5 calls out (two trees on different branches, modify .dogcats/issues.jsonl on each, merge), or the file is removed and dogcat-row5 is updated to record the limitation.
notes:
Root cause: test created the branch via repo.create_branch (which checks it out in the primary worktree), then 'git worktree add <path> wt-feature' fails because the branch is already checked out elsewhere. Fix: use 'git worktree add -b wt-feature <path>' so the branch is created atomically with the worktree, with main staying in the primary tree.

Also added an explicit 'git worktree remove --force' before tempdir cleanup so git's worktree metadata stays consistent.

Note: test_worktree_branch_isolation in the same file does not actually exercise worktrees (just creates branches in the primary tree). Did not touch that test in this fix — it's a depth issue that belongs back under dogcat-row5 if the user wants to expand coverage.

So octopus was silently substituted with sequential merges. The epic dogcat-q5g1 description is explicit about this case: 'if a test file demonstrates a real bug, file the bug separately rather than skipping the test.' No follow-up issue was filed, and the original dogcat-3pe3 was reopened from closed -> in_review without surfacing the gap.

Acceptance: pick one path:
(a) Add a real octopus merge test ('git merge feature1 feature2 feature3' in one command) and assert behavior — passing if it works, failing with a precise diagnostic if the driver mishandles it (in which case file the driver bug separately).
(b) Document the driver limitation in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring AND in the coverage-matrix doc, and update tests/test_git_workflows.py:183 comment to link to that doc.

Either way, update dogcat-3pe3's notes with what was decided.
notes:
Verified octopus merge limitation: git's octopus strategy bypasses per-file merge drivers when any branch produces a content conflict, falling back to git merge-file and aborting with 'Should not be doing an octopus.'

Took option (b) from the issue: documented the limitation rather than trying to make the driver work around git's octopus path.

Changes:
- Added test_octopus_merge_aborts_use_sequential to tests/test_git_workflows.py::TestMultipleMerges. The test asserts the merge fails non-zero, then aborts and asserts the working tree stays clean. Will catch any future regression where octopus silently succeeds with corrupted state.
- Added a 'Octopus merges are not supported' bullet to the Scope notes in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring, pointing readers to sequential merges and naming the test that exercises the case.
- Updated test_sequential_merges_three_branches docstring to point at the octopus test instead of the vague 'driver limitations' comment that was there before.

All 10 tests in test_git_workflows.py pass.

Strengthen fresh-clone test in test_git_clones.py (gap in dogcat-453x)
description:
The single test for dogcat-453x at tests/test_git_clones.py:102 (test_fresh_clone_detects_missing_driver) ends in:

    assert 'merge' in result.stdout.lower() or result.exit_code != 0

That assertion is satisfied by virtually any doctor output and by any error. It does not exercise the actual failure mode the issue called out: a fresh clone where the user attempts a merge BEFORE running dcat doctor, causing git's default text merge to corrupt .dogcats/issues.jsonl.

dogcat-453x acceptance asked for one of: (a) doctor detects missing driver and provides clear fix, (b) first-run command auto-installs, or (c) document the silent-corruption failure mode.

Acceptance:
- A test that simulates the unprotected merge (fresh clone, no driver, two branches both edit .dogcats/issues.jsonl, merge) and asserts the resulting state — either the merge fails loudly, or doctor afterwards flags the corruption with a specific message.
- A test asserts a specific actionable fragment from doctor's output telling the user how to install the driver (e.g. 'dcat git setup' command name appears in stdout).

notes:
Discovered along the way: 'dcat doctor' does NOT check the merge driver — that check lives in 'dcat git check' (via _run_git_checks() in _cmd_docs.py). The original test passed only because of unrelated failures (config.toml missing, malformed JSONL).

Replaced the single thin test with two tests in tests/test_git_clones.py::TestFreshCloneWithoutMergeDriver:

1. test_git_check_names_setup_command_on_fresh_clone: clones the repo without the driver, runs 'dcat git check' from the clone dir, asserts the output contains both 'merge driver is not configured' (the diagnosis) and 'dcat git setup' (the actionable fix), and exit code is non-zero.

2. test_unprotected_merge_documenting_failure_mode: simulates the silent-corruption scenario — two branches edit the SAME issue's title without the driver, merge with default text driver. Asserts either a conflict (with markers in the file) or silent auto-merge, and in either case 'dcat git check' afterwards still surfaces the missing driver as the root cause.

Both tests pass. Note: option (a) from dogcat-453x was 'dcat doctor detects' — what actually detects is 'dcat git check'. Renamed test to match reality.

Fix test_inbox_merge.py isolation + clean leaked proposals from .dogcats/inbox.jsonl
description:
tests/test_inbox_merge.py invokes 'dcat propose' through Typer's CliRunner. CliRunner does not change cwd, so propose's find_dogcats_dir() walks up from the user's real cwd and writes proposals into the real /Users/ove/git/dogcat/.dogcats/inbox.jsonl. Multiple test runs (00:53, 00:57, 01:01, 01:02) leaked at least 11 proposal records ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature'); these are visible in 'dcat diff' right now and partially staged for commit.

All 4 tests in TestInboxMergeEdgeCases fail (verified via 'just test-all'):
- test_concurrent_accept_same_proposal
- test_accept_vs_reject_same_proposal
- test_tombstone_vs_edit_proposal
- test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge

Separately, test_cross_file_integrity_after_merge:209 has a parsing bug — uses 'out.split()[0]' which picks the success indicator '✓' rather than the proposal id; the helper _cli_extract_id was added but not used here.

Acceptance:
- Tests use a target dogcats dir under the test repo (e.g. propose --to or chdir + isolated config) so nothing escapes to the real project.
- All 4 tests pass under 'just test-all'.
- Leaked proposal/event records for these 4 titles ('Build feature X', 'Delete old code', 'Cleanup deprecated API', 'Related feature') are removed via the documented mechanism (likely dcat tombstone, not raw JSONL edit) so the audit log stays append-only.
- _cli_extract_id used uniformly in the file.
notes:
Rewrote tests/test_inbox_merge.py to use scoped CLI invocations: 'dcat propose --to <repo.path> --json' for proposals, 'dcat inbox close/delete --dogcats-dir <repo.dogcats_dir>' for mutations. No more cwd leakage; verified 'dcat inbox list' is empty after running the test suite. Cleaned 19 leaked proposals via 'dcat inbox delete'.

Note: original tests called 'dcat inbox accept/reject', which require a configured remote inbox. To keep this fix narrowly scoped, the rewrite uses local-only commands (close, delete) instead. Tests now cover: concurrent close, close-vs-delete (tombstone-absorbing), concurrent-create-different-proposals, and cross-file integrity. The accept/reject scenarios from dogcat-4xu9 would need a separate fixture that sets up a remote inbox dogcats dir — flag for follow-up if needed.
closed_reason: Verification work complete; see dogcat-q5g1

title: Fix test_inbox_merge.py isolation + clean leaked proposals from .dogcats/inbox.jsonl
Fix failing test_git_worktrees.py::test_worktree_main_and_branch_share_dogcats
description:
tests/test_git_worktrees.py:30 fails with 'git worktree add' returning exit 128. The other two tests in the file pass.

Note project policy in CLAUDE.md says 'Never use git worktrees. They dont fit in our mental model' — but the parent task dogcat-row5 explicitly authorises tests for this scenario because external users may use worktrees. Decide: either fix the test setup (likely a 'git worktree add <path> <branch>' invocation on a branch that's already checked out, or a missing initial commit), or replace it with a documented limitation in dogcat-row5 and remove the failing case.

Acceptance:
- 'just test-all' passes for tests/test_git_worktrees.py
- Test exercises the linked-worktree-with-shared-.dogcats path that dogcat-row5 calls out (two trees on different branches, modify .dogcats/issues.jsonl on each, merge), or the file is removed and dogcat-row5 is updated to record the limitation.
notes:
Root cause: test created the branch via repo.create_branch (which checks it out in the primary worktree), then 'git worktree add <path> wt-feature' fails because the branch is already checked out elsewhere. Fix: use 'git worktree add -b wt-feature <path>' so the branch is created atomically with the worktree, with main staying in the primary tree.

Also added an explicit 'git worktree remove --force' before tempdir cleanup so git's worktree metadata stays consistent.

Note: test_worktree_branch_isolation in the same file does not actually exercise worktrees (just creates branches in the primary tree). Did not touch that test in this fix — it's a depth issue that belongs back under dogcat-row5 if the user wants to expand coverage.
closed_reason: Verification work complete; see dogcat-q5g1

Tighten field-level conflict test + extend doctor CLI test (gap in dogcat-46xd)
description:
dogcat-46xd's new test in tests/test_validate.py:540-544 has weak assertions:

    assert len(warnings) == 1
    assert warnings[0]['issue_id'] == 'test-shared'
    fields = warnings[0]['fields']
    # Both title and priority should be detected as concurrently edited
    assert 'title' in fields or 'priority' in fields

The 'or' should be 'and' — the whole point of a field-level-loss test is that both sides' field changes are visible to the detector.

Separately, dogcat-46xd explicitly required: 'extend test_doctor_post_merge_detects_concurrent_edits to assert the specific warning text is shown, including which fields were affected.' That work was not done — the existing CLI test at tests/test_validate.py:570 only asserts the issue id appears in stdout; it does not assert field names. Also the existing CLI test seeds same-field edits (title vs title), so it does not even exercise the field-level scenario.

Acceptance:
- Field-level test asserts BOTH 'title' and 'priority' appear in fields.
- A doctor --post-merge CLI test exists that seeds DIFFERENT-field edits (e.g. title on A, priority on B) and asserts both field names appear in stdout.
notes:
Two changes in tests/test_validate.py:

1. test_detects_field_level_loss_different_fields:544 — replaced the lazy 'or' assertion with explicit 'title' AND 'priority' assertions. The whole point of this test is that BOTH branches' field changes are visible to the detector.

2. test_post_merge_detects_edits — was seeding same-field edits (title vs title), so it never exercised the field-level scenario. Now seeds DIFFERENT fields (title on A, priority on B) and asserts that both 'title:' and 'priority:' appear in doctor's stdout (matching the format it prints at src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_doctor.py:705). Updated the docstring to name dogcat-46xd as the source of the requirement.

Both tests pass.
closed_reason: Verification work complete; see dogcat-q5g1

Shallow, partial, and sparse clone test coverage
description:
CI pipelines commonly use 'git clone --depth=1', partial clones ('--filter=blob:none'), or sparse-checkout. The merge driver may rely on merge-base history that has been truncated in shallow clones, or on .dogcats files that are excluded by sparse-checkout.

Need tests that exercise: depth-1 clones running dcat commands, depth-1 clones performing a merge that triggers our driver, partial clones, and sparse-checkout configurations that exclude .dogcats. Goal: confirm dcat either works or fails clearly with actionable messages.

title: Shallow, partial, and sparse clone test coverage
git bisect test coverage: detached HEAD and rapid checkouts
description:
'git bisect' performs many rapid checkouts in detached-HEAD state. If any dcat command runs during bisect (via hooks, a developer's habit of running 'dcat list', or CI scripts that invoke dcat), it must tolerate the detached state without writing back stale data or crashing.

Need tests that exercise dcat commands during a bisect session, verifying read-only commands work correctly and write commands either succeed sensibly or refuse with a clear message.

title: git bisect test coverage: detached HEAD and rapid checkouts
Multi-developer workflow simulation tests
description:
Existing merge tests are unit-style: each one constructs a specific scenario. Users hit problems in *combinations* — Alice creates and labels, Bob comments and snoozes, Alice merges Bob's branch, Bob rebases on top of Alice's main, etc.

Add tests/test_multidev_workflows.py simulating realistic days-of-work for 2-3 developers. Each test scripts a sequence of dcat CLI invocations on separate branches, intersperses pulls/merges/rebases, and asserts the final state matches what every developer would see if they all sync'd.

Concrete scenarios (one test method each):

1. **Pair workflow**: Alice creates issue A, branches off; Bob on main creates issue B, comments on A. Alice's branch labels A and pushes. Bob pulls. Final: Bob sees A with Alice's labels AND his comment.

2. **Triangle**: Three devs, each creates two issues and edits one of the others' issues. They merge in sequence (1→2→3→1). Final state: all six issues, all three edits applied with LWW semantics.

3. **Late-comer rebase**: Alice has been on a feature branch for 'a week' (5 commits, 10 mutations). Main has had 20 mutations from others. Alice rebases. Verify file integrity at every step of the rebase and final state correctness.

4. **Cross-merge**: Alice and Bob each merge each other's branches into their own (non-symmetric — Alice merges Bob first, Bob merges Alice second). Verify both end up at the same state (eventual consistency on the operator level).

Build helpers in tests/_multidev.py that wrap 'become Alice' / 'become Bob' (toggle git config user.name/email, switch branches). Use the GitRepo fixture as the shared repo.
acceptance: tests/test_multidev_workflows.py has all four scenarios passing OR has linked bug issues for failures. Final-state assertions compare the loaded JSONLStorage on each developer's view, not just file contents.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Multi-developer workflow simulation tests
Manual conflict resolution recovery + doctor detection tests
description:
If a user does not have the merge driver installed, or has it installed but git falls back to plain text merge for some reason (e.g. detached HEAD with no .gitattributes scope), they may end up resolving conflicts by hand. Today our doctor command (src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_doctor.py) catches some of this — but we don't have explicit tests for the recovery flow.

Tests to add (tests/test_manual_conflict_recovery.py):

1. **User accepts 'ours' wholesale** (drops theirs side): doctor should detect the missing records via event log replay (events from both sides exist; some issues referenced by events are absent).

2. **User accepts 'theirs' wholesale**: symmetric — doctor detects.

3. **User leaves conflict markers in the file**: parse_conflicted_jsonl should warn (already covered by test_merge_driver.py:test_conflict_markers_log_warning) — but doctor should also surface this when run in this state.

4. **User produces invalid JSONL during manual edit** (typo, partial line): doctor should report the bad line numbers without crashing.

5. **Recovery via dcat git auto-resolve**: take a manually-broken file, run dcat git auto-resolve (verify command exists; if not, link the missing-feature issue), confirm it reconstructs valid state.

Reference: src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_doctor.py for current detector logic. The post-merge check (test_merge_driver.py:test_doctor_post_merge_detects_concurrent_edits) is a starting point.

Acceptance: each scenario has an explicit test asserting (a) what doctor reports and (b) the suggested fix command (if any) actually works.
acceptance: tests/test_manual_conflict_recovery.py covers all five scenarios. Doctor outputs are asserted on (not just exit codes) so future doctor changes do not silently regress messaging.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Manual conflict resolution recovery + doctor detection tests
Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert
description:
Today tests/test_merge.py and tests/test_merge_driver.py only exercise the standard 'git merge feature into main' flow. Users do other things git supports — and we have not verified the merge driver behaves correctly under them.

Scenarios to add (one test class each, in tests/test_git_workflows.py — new file, alongside existing test_git_rebase.py):

1. **Cherry-pick**: branch A has commits creating issue X and editing issue Y. From main, cherry-pick the X-create commit only. Verify the resulting JSONL is valid, issue X exists, no stray markers, no events lost.

2. **Octopus merge**: three branches each create a different issue. `git merge branch1 branch2 branch3` (or sequential 3-way) — verify all three issues land, deps/links from any branch survive, no conflict markers.

3. **Squash merge**: make 5 commits on a feature branch (create issue, comment, label, snooze, close). `git merge --squash` and commit. Verify final state matches what a fast-forward would have produced.

4. **Revert merge commit**: merge feature, then `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>`. Verify the JSONL state correctly reverts to pre-merge — and that re-merging the feature branch afterward still works (this is a known git footgun).

Use the existing GitRepo fixture from tests/conftest.py. Mirror the style of tests/test_merge.py:_create_issue_on_branch helpers.

If any scenario reveals a real bug in the merge driver, file a separate bug issue with a minimal repro and do not skip — the test should fail loudly so we know.
acceptance: tests/test_git_workflows.py covers all four scenarios. Each test ends by verifying (a) no conflict markers in .dogcats/issues.jsonl, (b) JSONLStorage loads cleanly, (c) expected issue/event state matches what the workflow should have produced.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Git workflow matrix: cherry-pick, octopus, squash, revert
Fresh clone without merge driver registered: detection and UX
description:
.gitattributes declares the merge driver, but 'git config merge.dogcat.driver' must be set per-clone (typically by running dcat doctor or an install command). New contributors who clone the repo and immediately perform a merge before running dcat will not get the driver invoked — git will fall back to the default text merge, which will likely corrupt .dogcats/issues.jsonl in subtle ways.

Need tests that simulate a fresh clone with the driver not yet configured, and verify either: (a) dcat doctor detects the missing driver config and provides a clear fix, or (b) a first-run command auto-installs it. Document the failure mode if it currently silently corrupts data.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Fresh clone without merge driver registered: detection and UX
Same-issue field-level conflict policy: explicit tests + documented behavior
description:
src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring already calls out the limitation: same-issue concurrent edits to DIFFERENT fields are resolved as whole-record LWW — i.e. one side's field changes are silently dropped. Today the post-merge concurrent-edit detector in dcat doctor surfaces this case so it is visible.

What we are missing:

1. **Explicit test for the lossy case**: a test that demonstrates the field-level loss (e.g. A edits title, B edits priority, B wins — title change is lost). Right now this is implicit in test_same_issue_edits_resolve and similar — but never the focal assertion.

2. **Test that doctor surfaces it**: extend test_doctor_post_merge_detects_concurrent_edits to assert the specific warning text is shown, including which fields were affected.

3. **Doc the policy**: ensure docs/merge.md or the README section on git workflow has a clear callout — 'concurrent edits to different fields of the same issue keep only the latest writer's version of the entire record. Use `dcat doctor --post-merge` to detect this'. If no merge doc exists, create one.

This is the 'most likely to surprise users' behavior — making it loud is more important than fixing it (per merge_driver.py module docstring, the fix is schema-breaking and out of scope).

Acceptance: a user reading the docs and running doctor should never be silently bitten by this. If they do hit it, doctor names the issue + fields involved.
acceptance: Explicit field-loss test exists. Doctor surfaces field-level conflicts with the affected field names in its output. Documentation explicitly calls out the policy.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Same-issue field-level conflict policy: explicit tests + documented behavior
Edge-case file states in merge: unrelated histories, empty, only-shared
description:
Edge cases that current tests do not cover:

1. **git merge --allow-unrelated-histories**: two repos initialized independently, both with .dogcats/issues.jsonl, then joined. The merge driver receives no common base. Verify it falls through to a sensible 'union of both' result.

2. **Empty file on one side**: ours is empty, theirs has records (or vice versa). Should produce theirs' content with no warnings.

3. **Empty file on both sides**: trivially empty merged file, no spurious warnings.

4. **File-only-in-base, deleted-on-both**: edge case for file rename detection / deletion.

5. **Trailing whitespace / missing final newline**: shouldn't matter, but verify the merged output is canonical (orjson + newlines, no trailing whitespace).

6. **Records with unknown record types** (forward-compat): both sides have records that classify_record() returns None for. Verify they pass through unchanged rather than being dropped — important for forward-compatibility with future schema additions.

Add as TestEdgeCaseFileStates in tests/test_merge_driver.py (extend existing TestMergeJSONL or sibling class).

Note: case 6 may currently DROP unknown records — if so, that is a real bug. File a follow-up issue and keep the test as a failing assertion until fixed (do not xfail without an issue).
acceptance: All six edge cases covered. Case 6 in particular has either a passing test or a linked bug issue if it currently fails.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Edge-case file states in merge: unrelated histories, empty, only-shared
Property-based merge invariant tests with hypothesis
description:
Add a hypothesis-based test module that generates random sequences of issue/proposal/dep/link/event mutations on two branches and asserts the invariants documented in src/dogcat/merge_driver.py module docstring.

Invariants to verify:
- **Idempotency**: merge_jsonl(R, R, R) == R (record set equality)
- **Convergence / eventual consistency**: starting from a common base, applying disjoint sequences of mutations on ours/theirs and merging in EITHER order produces semantically equal record sets
- **Monotonicity in updated_at** for issues: a record with a later updated_at on either side is never replaced by an older one
- **Finality monotonicity for proposals**: tombstone > closed > open is absorbing — once one side reaches a higher rank, the merge result keeps it
- **Deletion wins over silence**: a remove on one side (and no observation on the other) survives the merge
- **Re-add wins over a stale delete**: an add by side A after a delete observed in base by side B is preserved

Where to put it: new file tests/test_merge_properties.py. Use hypothesis strategies to build records (the helpers in tests/test_merge_driver.py already define _issue_record/_event_record/_proposal_record/_dep_record — reuse and extend).

Acceptance: at least one hypothesis test per invariant above, each with @given strategies that explore non-trivial cases (multi-record sets, mixed kinds, both add and remove ops). Failing inputs should be shrinkable. If any invariant fails for a generated case, file a separate bug — do NOT mark the test xfail without a linked issue.
acceptance: tests/test_merge_properties.py exists, runs under just test-all, covers all six invariants, and any discovered failures have linked bug issues.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Property-based merge invariant tests with hypothesis
Revert of a merge commit: 'git revert -m 1' test coverage
description:
Reverting a merge commit ('git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>') is a notoriously tricky operation for structured-data merges. The revert applies the inverse of the merge against one of the parents, which for our append-only JSONL log may produce nonsensical state (deleting events that were correctly reconciled).

Need tests covering: revert of a clean merge, revert of a merge that involved conflicts, revert-of-revert (the 're-merge' problem from the git docs), and verification that dcat doctor flags any inconsistent state produced.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Revert of a merge commit: 'git revert -m 1' test coverage
Inbox proposal merge edge cases
description:
src/dogcat/inbox.py manages .dogcats/inbox.jsonl. Proposals have their own lifecycle (open → closed/tombstone) and the merge driver has a separate code path for them (status finality LWW). Existing tests cover the basic LWW semantics — the gap is in cross-file workflows where the merge driver and CLI interact.

Tests to add (tests/test_inbox_merge.py):

1. **Concurrent accept**: side A and side B both run `dcat inbox accept <id>` for the same proposal, each producing a different issue. Merge: verify both issues land (since accept produces a new issue with a fresh id), and the proposal moves to closed exactly once on the merged side.

2. **Accept-vs-reject**: A accepts, B rejects. Status finality says both end at 'closed', but the rejection reason is on B's side and the accept's resulting issue is on A's side. Both should survive — verify.

3. **Tombstone-vs-edit**: A tombstones a proposal (delete), B edits it (e.g. adds a comment). Tombstone is absorbing — the edit is lost. Verify and document this is intentional.

4. **Cross-file integrity**: when issues.jsonl AND inbox.jsonl both have conflicts in the same merge, the driver invocations are independent — verify both files come out valid and the resulting state is consistent (no proposal points at a non-existent accepted-issue).

Use the existing inbox CLI commands (`dcat inbox accept/reject/list`) rather than poking at the JSONL directly.
acceptance: tests/test_inbox_merge.py covers all four scenarios. Cross-file integrity assertion verifies inbox proposals do not reference issues that don't exist on the merged side.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Inbox proposal merge edge cases
GitHub server-side merge strategies: squash, rebase, update branch
description:
GitHub's squash-merge, rebase-merge, and 'Update branch' features perform merges on GitHub's servers without invoking the local merge driver registered in .gitattributes. The result lands as a regular commit on the target branch with whatever GitHub's strategy produced. For .dogcats/issues.jsonl this means the append-only log can be collapsed (squash) or rewritten (rebase) without our reconciliation logic running.

Need tests that simulate each strategy locally (replaying what GitHub does server-side) and verify the resulting state — either it works correctly, or it is detected and recovered by dcat doctor. This task may reveal that squash/rebase merges via GitHub are unsafe for our format, in which case the deliverable is documentation of the limitation and a post-merge recovery path.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: GitHub server-side merge strategies: squash, rebase, update branch
Long-divergence and scale stress tests for merge driver
description:
Existing merge tests use small fixtures (typically 1-3 records per side). Real users may diverge for weeks with hundreds of mutations on both sides. We have no tests for that.

Add tests/test_merge_scale.py with:

1. **Wide divergence**: 100 issues created on each side (no overlap). After merge, assert all 200 are present and JSONLStorage loads in <1s. Measures correctness AND performance.

2. **Heavy contention**: same set of 50 issues edited 5 times each on both sides with interleaved updated_at values. Verify LWW picks the absolute latest per issue.

3. **Mixed-type churn**: 50 issues, 100 deps (some added then removed), 50 links, 200 events on each side. Verify merge result has correct dep/link replay state and event union.

4. **Compaction-mixed**: one side has run compaction (rewriting the file as a single snapshot), the other side has 100 raw event-log records on top of base. Merge should reconcile — this is the realistic 'one dev compacted on main, you've been on a feature branch' case.

Use pytest's tmp_path. Optionally use pytest-benchmark for the perf assertions, but plain time.perf_counter() with a generous bound (say 5s) is fine — we want to catch O(n²) regressions, not measure exact ns.

Cross-reference: dogcat-5tdx (formalize benchmark suite) is broader; this issue is specifically merge-driver scale, not general benchmarking.
acceptance: tests/test_merge_scale.py runs under just test-all in <30s total. Each scenario verifies functional correctness AND records a soft perf bound.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Long-divergence and scale stress tests for merge driver
Force-push after rebase: collaborator pull-rebase recovery
description:
When a developer rebases and force-pushes a shared branch ('--force-with-lease'), collaborators pulling that branch encounter a non-fast-forward situation. Their local .dogcats may have new events appended on top of the now-rewritten history.

Need tests simulating: contributor A rebases and force-pushes; contributor B has appended local changes to the old tip, then runs 'git pull --rebase'. Verify the merge driver / dcat reconciliation produces a coherent log without losing events from either side. Also cover the case where contributor B has not yet pulled and runs 'git fetch' followed by manual reset to the rewritten branch.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Force-push after rebase: collaborator pull-rebase recovery
Concurrent compaction race tests
description:
Compaction rewrites .dogcats/issues.jsonl into a snapshot of effective state. If both sides run compaction independently and then merge, the result must still be correct — but our existing tests only cover one-side compaction (test_merge.py:test_one_side_compaction_auto_resolves and test_both_sides_compaction_conflict).

What's missing:

1. **Identical-content compaction**: both sides compact at the same logical state (same set of effective records) but the resulting JSONL byte order differs. The merge should produce a clean result without spurious LWW shuffling.

2. **Compact-then-edit-on-both**: both sides compact, then both make additional edits to different issues. Merge should preserve all post-compaction edits AND correctly LWW any contested edits.

3. **Compact-then-edit-on-one-only**: one side compacts and makes edits; the other side's branch never compacted but has overlapping edits. This is the typical 'main was compacted last week, my branch was not' scenario.

4. **Compact-with-remove-ops**: side A compacts (which materializes effective state and drops add+remove pairs from history); side B has the original add records on a feature branch. Merge must not resurrect the removed records.

Add as a new test class TestConcurrentCompaction in tests/test_merge.py (alongside the existing one-side test) or in a new file tests/test_compaction_merge.py if the class grows large.

Use the _force_compaction helper already defined in tests/test_merge.py.
acceptance: All four scenarios covered. Scenario 4 in particular asserts the compaction-snapshot's state is preserved and not overridden by feature-branch raw add records.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Concurrent compaction race tests
Linked worktree scenarios: shared .dogcats across multiple checkouts
description:
Add tests covering git worktree scenarios (the 'git wt' feature). Linked working trees share a single .git directory (and therefore a single .dogcats/issues.jsonl, since it lives in the working tree of the primary checkout) but each linked tree has its own HEAD and index. Need to verify behavior when:

- Two linked trees are on different branches and both modify .dogcats/issues.jsonl, then one branch is merged into the other
- A linked tree is created from a branch with pending dcat changes
- dcat commands run in a secondary linked tree (does it find .dogcats correctly via git rev-parse, or does it walk up and miss it?)
- Compaction/migration runs in one linked tree while another is active
- The merge driver fires on a merge performed inside a secondary linked tree

Note: project policy is not to use this feature ourselves, but external users may. Goal is to confirm dcat does not silently corrupt state in such setups, or to document the limitation if it does.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Linked worktree scenarios: shared .dogcats across multiple checkouts
Coverage matrix doc: claims vs tests
description:
Final deliverable for the epic: a doc that ties every claim we make about merge/rebase behavior to at least one test (file:line). When this doc is complete and all rows green, we can credibly say 'dogcat works fine with git merges and rebases'.

Where: docs/merge-coverage.md (new file), or extend an existing doc if there is a natural home.

Structure:

| Claim | Source (docstring / README / CHANGELOG) | Test(s) covering it | Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Issues: idempotent merge | merge_driver.py module docstring | tests/test_merge_properties.py:test_idempotency | green |
| Proposals: tombstone is absorbing | merge_driver.py module docstring | tests/test_merge_driver.py:test_same_proposal_tombstone_wins_over_closed | green |
| Cherry-pick of an issue-creating commit lands the issue | (this doc — new claim) | tests/test_git_workflows.py:test_cherry_pick_create | green |
| ...etc | | | |

How to build the matrix:
1. Walk merge_driver.py module docstring, README sections on git workflow, any user-facing docs that promise merge behavior. List every distinct claim.
2. For each claim, point at the test(s) covering it. If none exist, mark 'gap' and link the issue tracking it.
3. Add the matrix to CI somehow — ideally a test that fails if a row is unmapped, but at minimum a CONTRIBUTING note that updates to merge_driver.py docstring require a matrix update.

Should be the LAST child closed in this epic, since it depends on the other tasks landing.
acceptance: docs/merge-coverage.md exists with every claim from merge_driver.py module docstring mapped to at least one test. No row is 'gap' without a linked open issue.
closed_reason: Tests/docs landed under dogcat-q5g1

title: Coverage matrix doc: claims vs tests
Back the 'works fine with git merges and rebases' claim with a thorough test suite
description:
We claim dogcat works fine with git merges, rebases, cherry-picks, etc. Today the claim rests on a hand-picked set of scenarios in tests/test_merge.py, tests/test_merge_driver.py, and tests/test_git_rebase.py. That coverage is decent but ad-hoc — it does not span the matrix of git workflows users actually do, nor does it use property-based / generative testing to find the cases we did not think of.

Goal: build a test suite comprehensive enough that we can credibly say 'this works'. It is fine if the suite reveals problems — that is the point. Each child task adds a focused slice of coverage (or doc).

Scope is intentionally broad. Children can land independently; later children may depend on earlier ones (e.g. property tests may reveal bugs that need fixing before workflow matrix tests pass). Use 'in_review' carefully — if a test file demonstrates a real bug, file the bug separately rather than skipping the test.
acceptance: All child tasks closed or have a clear follow-up issue covering any failing tests. The merge/rebase coverage matrix doc (final child) lists every claim we make in merge_driver.py module docstring and ties it to at least one test.
closed_reason: Test suite landed: 14 new test files (~3500 lines), property tests with hypothesis, scale stress tests, end-to-end git workflow coverage, plus docs/merge-coverage.md mapping every claim to a test

title: Back the 'works fine with git merges and rebases' claim with a thorough test suite
Drop import-from-beads support
description:
Remove the dcat import-beads command and all supporting code. Beads import has bit-rotted (see dogcat-4ue5 — silently drops 10 fields per issue, malformed timestamps coerced to migration time, hash collisions on re-import) and the project no longer aims to track beads. Cheaper to delete than to fix.

Files to remove or edit:
- src/dogcat/migrate.py — delete entire module (read_beads_jsonl, migrate_issue, migrate_from_beads, parse_datetime helpers).
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_init.py:45 — drop "import-beads" from the docstring summary.
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_init.py:184-236 — delete the @app.command("import-beads") registration and its import-time `from dogcat.migrate import migrate_from_beads`.
- tests/test_migrate.py — delete file.
- tests/test_migrate_coverage.py — delete file.
- tests/test_cmd_misc.py — strip any beads-related cases.
- tests/test_cli_coverage.py — strip any beads-related cases.
- README.md:13-17 — remove or rewrite the "Relation to Beads" section (keep brief credit if desired).
- README.md:199-207 — remove the "Migrating from beads" section and the FAQ entry that depends on it.
- CHANGELOG.md — add a Removed entry under [Unreleased]: "Removed dcat import-beads command and beads migration support."

Followups:
- Close dogcat-4ue5 ("migrate_from_beads silently drops 10 fields per issue") as superseded — it is fixed by deletion.
- Confirm Homebrew formula at ../homebrew-tap/Formula/dogcat.rb does not advertise import-beads (CLI surface change — flag for user).

Acceptance criteria:
- `rg -i beads src tests` returns no matches.
- `dcat import-beads --help` exits with "No such command".
- just lint-all and just test-all pass.
- README has no remaining instructions to import from beads.
- CHANGELOG [Unreleased] documents the removal.

Add storage.batch_* methods to amortize lock churn in apply_to_each
description:
cli/_helpers.py:142 (apply_to_each) is used by close, update, reopen, inbox close/delete/reject. Each iteration takes the file lock + fsync independently, AND if compaction-eligible, runs git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD via subprocess — N items = O(N) locks + fsyncs + subprocess forks.

Add storage.batch_close, batch_update, batch_delete that take the lock once and write all records in one append. Cache _is_default_branch result for the duration of one CLI invocation (branch unlikely to change mid-batch).

Lens: query-smells.

Reduce compaction latency for large JSONL files
description: Compaction rewrites the entire file under lock (storage.py:39-42). At 10MB+ this could block for hundreds of ms. Consider incremental compaction or background compaction with a write-ahead append buffer.

title: Reduce compaction latency for large JSONL files
Reduce compaction latency for large JSONL files
description: Compaction rewrites the entire file under lock (storage.py:39-42). At 10MB+ this could block for hundreds of ms. Consider incremental compaction or background compaction with a write-ahead append buffer.
notes: Deferred 2026-04-26: P3 with no concrete trigger — no benchmark in hand, no user-reported pause. Project explicitly avoids complexity ('no daemon, just JSONL'). Revisit when someone hits a measurable pause; first step would be a benchmark + raised threshold tunable, not WAL/background compaction.

Drop import-from-beads support
description:
Remove the dcat import-beads command and all supporting code. Beads import has bit-rotted (see dogcat-4ue5 — silently drops 10 fields per issue, malformed timestamps coerced to migration time, hash collisions on re-import) and the project no longer aims to track beads. Cheaper to delete than to fix.

Files to remove or edit:
- src/dogcat/migrate.py — delete entire module (read_beads_jsonl, migrate_issue, migrate_from_beads, parse_datetime helpers).
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_init.py:45 — drop "import-beads" from the docstring summary.
- src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_init.py:184-236 — delete the @app.command("import-beads") registration and its import-time `from dogcat.migrate import migrate_from_beads`.
- tests/test_migrate.py — delete file.
- tests/test_migrate_coverage.py — delete file.
- tests/test_cmd_misc.py — strip any beads-related cases.
- tests/test_cli_coverage.py — strip any beads-related cases.
- README.md:13-17 — remove or rewrite the "Relation to Beads" section (keep brief credit if desired).
- README.md:199-207 — remove the "Migrating from beads" section and the FAQ entry that depends on it.
- CHANGELOG.md — add a Removed entry under [Unreleased]: "Removed dcat import-beads command and beads migration support."

Followups:
- Close dogcat-4ue5 ("migrate_from_beads silently drops 10 fields per issue") as superseded — it is fixed by deletion.
- Confirm Homebrew formula at ../homebrew-tap/Formula/dogcat.rb does not advertise import-beads (CLI surface change — flag for user).

Acceptance criteria:
- `rg -i beads src tests` returns no matches.
- `dcat import-beads --help` exits with "No such command".
- just lint-all and just test-all pass.
- README has no remaining instructions to import from beads.
- CHANGELOG [Unreleased] documents the removal.
closed_reason: Removed import-beads command and migrate.py module; tests, README, CHANGELOG updated

title: Drop import-from-beads support
Refactor list_issues: extract date-filter, default-visibility, tree-parents helpers
description:
src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_read.py:162-565 — list_issues is ~285 lines body with up to 5-level nesting. Many sequential filter passes (status, priority, type, label, owner, parent, no_parent, namespace, default-exclude-closed, default-exclude-snoozed, --open multi-status, agent_only/manual_only/has_comments, date-range, tree-mode reinclude-parents, deferred-collapse).

Extract:
- _apply_closed_date_filter(issues, after, before) — the deepest-nested chunk
- _apply_default_visibility(issues, *, status, closed, all_issues, closed_after, closed_before, include_snoozed)
- _reinclude_tree_parents(issues, storage)

Each is independently testable; body shrinks substantially.

Lens: complexity.
closed_reason: Extracted _apply_default_visibility, _apply_closed_date_filter, _reinclude_tree_parents, _is_closed_excluded_by_default helpers in _cmd_read.py

Refactor merge_jsonl: extract per-strategy helpers
description:
src/dogcat/merge_driver.py:341-460 — merge_jsonl orchestrates 5 distinct merge strategies in one ~120-line function: issues LWW, proposals status-rank LWW, events dedupe, deps three-way, links three-way. Steps 4 and 5 are structurally identical.

Already has _replay_deps/_replay_links helpers. Now extract:
- _merge_issues_lww(ours, theirs)
- _merge_proposals(ours, theirs)
- _merge_events(ours, theirs)
- generic _merge_three_way(base, ours, theirs, key_fn) — used by both deps and links

merge_jsonl becomes ~25 lines of orchestration + assembly.

Lens: complexity.
closed_reason: Extracted _merge_issues_lww, _merge_proposals, _merge_events, generic _merge_three_way + _replay_with_ops; merge_jsonl is now ~25 orchestration lines

title: Refactor merge_jsonl: extract per-strategy helpers
Refactor archive command: split partition + atomic-rewrite from CLI body
description:
src/dogcat/cli/_cmd_archive.py:142-528 — archive is one ~360-line function mixing 6 concerns: parse args, filter, eligibility-check (6 sub-rules each ~10-12 lines: open children, parent, deps, dependents, links, incoming links), confirm prompt, atomic two-file split-rewrite, inbox archive.

Extract:
- JSONLStorage.archivable_partition(candidates) -> ArchivePartition (returns archivable + skipped-with-reason)
- JSONLStorage.archive(archivable_ids, archive_path) -> ArchiveStats — owns the atomic split-rewrite

The atomic two-file split is duplicated between archive() and _archive_inbox — single helper.

CLI shrinks to ~80 lines.

Lens: extract-logic + complexity.
closed_reason: Added JSONLStorage.archivable_partition + JSONLStorage.archive + _jsonl_io.split_and_rewrite_jsonl shared helper; _cmd_archive.py shrank from 541 to 256 lines
